metadata.title: Shallow Review of AI Governance for Catastrophic & Existential Risk, 2025

organizations.name: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
organizations.description: Oxford-based research centre studying AI governance challenges, directed by Ben Garfinkel. Originally founded within the Future of Humanity Institute by Allan Dafoe.

organizations.name: MIRI Technical Governance Team
organizations.description: MIRI's governance arm focused on technical questions bearing on AI regulation and policy, advocating for building global Off Switch infrastructure to enable a coordinated halt in frontier AI development.

organizations.name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
organizations.description: Georgetown University policy research centre producing evidence-driven analysis on security implications of emerging technologies including AI.

organizations.name: Future of Humanity Institute (FHI)
organizations.description: Multidisciplinary research institute at Oxford University founded by Nick Bostrom, focused on big-picture questions for human civilization. Closed in 2024; many researchers continued at GovAI and other organizations.

organizations.name: Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
organizations.description: San Francisco-based nonprofit founded by Dan Hendrycks and Oliver Zhang in 2022, conducting safety research, building the field, and advocating for safety standards. Published the 2023 statement on AI extinction risk.

organizations.name: Future of Life Institute (FLI)
organizations.description: Nonprofit working to reduce large-scale risks from transformative technologies, particularly AI. Published the 2023 Pause letter and conducts policy research and advocacy.

organizations.name: RAND Corporation
organizations.description: Global policy think tank producing research on AI governance, security, and catastrophic risk, including frontier AI security and biological weapons risk.

organizations.name: Brookings Institution AI Initiative
organizations.description: Policy research initiative advancing good governance of transformative technologies through original research and strategic convening.

organizations.name: Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR)
organizations.description: UK-based think tank working to transform global resilience to extreme risks, focusing on AI risk, biological risk, and government risk management.

organizations.name: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS)
organizations.description: Nonpartisan think tank producing policy research on AI implications from today's models to potential AGI, based in Washington, D.C.

organizations.name: Convergence Analysis
organizations.description: Non-profit research organization building foundational reports on key AI scenarios and governance recommendations.

organizations.name: Epoch AI
organizations.description: Research institute investigating key trends and questions shaping the trajectory and governance of AI, collecting data on compute, models, and hardware.

organizations.name: Partnership on AI (PAI)
organizations.description: Multi-stakeholder organization developing responsible AI practices, bringing together industry, civil society, and academia.

organizations.name: OECD AI Policy Observatory
organizations.description: OECD platform providing data, analysis, and policy guidance on AI, operating the AI incidents monitor and catalogue of trustworthy AI tools.

organizations.name: AI Now Institute
organizations.description: Independent policy research institute studying the concentration of power in the tech industry and advocating for accountability in AI governance.

organizations.name: Ada Lovelace Institute
organizations.description: UK-based research institute examining how data and AI affect people and society, producing policy-focused research on AI regulation.

organizations.name: METR (Model Evaluation and Threat Research)
organizations.description: Berkeley-based nonprofit evaluating frontier AI models for dangerous capabilities, conducting pre-deployment evaluations for major AI labs.

categories.name: Compute Governance
categories.description: Governing access to and use of computational resources as a lever for AI safety — chip controls, export restrictions, compute monitoring, and licensing regimes.

agendas.name: Compute Monitoring & Verification
agendas.description: Technical and institutional mechanisms for tracking large-scale compute usage to detect dangerous AI training runs.

papers.title: Computing Power and the Governance of Artificial Intelligence
papers.description: Landmark paper by 19 researchers arguing that compute is detectable, excludable, and quantifiable, making it a particularly effective governance lever for AI.

papers.title: Governing Through the Cloud: The Intermediary Role of Compute Providers in AI Regulation
papers.description: Argues compute providers should serve as intermediaries for AI regulation, acting as securers, record keepers, verifiers, and enforcers.

papers.title: What Does it Take to Catch a Chinchilla? Verifying Rules on Large-Scale Neural Network Training via Compute Monitoring
papers.description: Proposes technical mechanisms for verifying compliance with rules on large-scale neural network training.

papers.title: Oversight for Frontier AI through a Know-Your-Customer Scheme for Compute Providers
papers.description: Proposes a Know-Your-Customer scheme for compute providers to create oversight mechanisms for frontier AI development.

papers.title: What is Compute Governance?
papers.description: Foundational explainer of why compute is uniquely governable — physical, concentrated supply chain, measurable — and the main policy levers available.

papers.title: Distributed and Decentralised Training: Technical Governance Challenges
papers.description: Identifies 'compute structuring' risks from distributed training. Current frontier runs need 100+ MW, making covert training very difficult — for now.

agendas.research_direction: Develop technical and institutional mechanisms for tracking large-scale compute usage worldwide. The core insight is that compute — unlike algorithms or data — is physical, concentrated in supply chains, and quantifiable. This makes it the most governable input to AI development. Research in this agenda designs systems that can detect unauthorized large training runs, verify compliance with compute caps, and create a governance layer at the infrastructure level.
agendas.theory_of_change: If governments can reliably monitor who is using how much compute for what purpose, they gain the ability to enforce rules on AI development without needing to understand the AI itself. This is analogous to how nuclear nonproliferation works through monitoring enrichment facilities rather than understanding warhead designs. Compute monitoring is the foundation on which most other governance proposals (licensing, international agreements, safety thresholds) ultimately rest.
agendas.progress: The field has made significant conceptual progress. [Heim et al. (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.08797) established compute as a governance lever with a landmark 19-author paper. [Shavit (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11341) proposed technical verification mechanisms. [GovAI](https://www.governance.ai)'s [KYC scheme for compute providers](https://www.governance.ai/research-paper/oversight-for-frontier-ai-through-a-know-your-customer-scheme) is being discussed in policy circles. The [Biden Executive Order](https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/presidential-actions/2023/10/30/executive-order-on-the-safe-secure-and-trustworthy-development-and-use-of-artificial-intelligence/)'s reporting threshold (10^26 FLOP) was the first regulatory use of compute as a governance metric. Cloud providers are beginning to implement customer verification. However, no country has yet deployed comprehensive compute monitoring at scale.

open_problems.problem: How to monitor on-premise compute that isn't routed through cloud providers
open_problems.best_answer: [Sastry, Heim et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.08797) propose layered monitoring: KYC for chip purchases, energy consumption monitoring (large runs consume 100+ MW, detectable via power signatures), and hardware-dependent tracking. But they acknowledge on-premise monitoring is far harder than cloud — energy monitoring misses smaller runs, and there's no solution for already-deployed hardware.
open_problems.source: Sastry, Heim et al., 'Computing Power and the Governance of AI,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Strong on cloud, weak on on-premise. The approach relies on chip supply chain chokepoints that erode as manufacturing diversifies. No convincing solution exists for monitoring hardware already deployed worldwide.

open_problems.problem: Whether compute monitoring can keep up with algorithmic efficiency gains that reduce compute requirements
open_problems.best_answer: [Epoch AI](https://epoch.ai/blog/algorithmic-progress-in-language-models) finds compute needed for a given capability halves every ~8 months. But [Besiroglu et al.](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.21622) (2025) argue that most algorithmic progress (2017-2025) came from two scale-dependent innovations (Transformers and Chinchilla scaling), suggesting progress may slow if compute scaling is constrained.
open_problems.source: Epoch AI, 'Algorithmic Progress in Language Models,' 2024; Besiroglu et al., 'On the Origin of Algorithmic Progress,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The finding that most progress is compute-dependent is somewhat reassuring for compute governance, but the confidence interval is wide. If compute-independent breakthroughs emerge, monitoring becomes much less effective.

open_problems.problem: How to set thresholds that are meaningful given rapidly changing capabilities per FLOP
open_problems.best_answer: The [Oxford AI Governance Institute](https://aigi.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/Survey_on_thresholds_for_advanced_AI_systems_1.pdf) (2025) argues for relative thresholds (e.g., percentage of the largest training run) rather than absolute FLOP values. The EU AI Act's 10^25 FLOP threshold already captures many non-frontier models. No proposal convincingly resolves the tension between predictability (labs need to plan) and adaptability (technology moves fast).
open_problems.source: Oxford AIGI, 'Survey on Thresholds for Advanced AI Systems,' August 2025
open_problems.critique: Relative thresholds are cleverer but require knowing what the largest run is, which is hard to verify. No proposal has been adopted in legislation.

open_problems.problem: Privacy and civil liberties implications of compute surveillance
open_problems.best_answer: [Lennart Heim](https://blog.heim.xyz/crucial-considerations-for-compute-governance/) (2024) argues targeting all compute is 'neither feasible nor desirable' and advocates narrowly scoped interventions on data center-scale AI chips only, combined with privacy-preserving verification (e.g., zero-knowledge proofs).
open_problems.source: Heim, 'Crucial Considerations for Compute Governance,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The most thoughtful treatment from the compute governance community, but only a blog post. Largely dismisses the concern by narrowing scope, ignoring mission creep and the surveillance studies literature. ACLU and EFF perspectives remain unaddressed.

open_problems.problem: Whether distributed training across many smaller clusters can evade monitoring
open_problems.best_answer: [Okolo et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.07765) (ICML 2025) provide the most rigorous treatment, identifying 'compute structuring' risks (splitting training across sub-threshold clusters). Current frontier runs require 100+ MW and specialized networking, making covert distributed training very difficult — for now. But efficiency improvements will lower this bar.
open_problems.source: Okolo et al., 'Distributed and Decentralised Training,' ICML 2025
open_problems.critique: Correctly identifies that frontier training is still hard to hide due to power and networking requirements. But the paper acknowledges the threshold is dropping. Missing: quantitative estimates of consumer GPU pooling feasibility.

open_problems.problem: International coordination on compute monitoring standards
open_problems.best_answer: The [UN HLAB-AI](https://www.un.org/sites/un2.un.org/files/governing_ai_for_humanity_final_report_en.pdf) (2024) proposes an international scientific panel and standards exchange. The Global Digital Compact followed in September 2024. But no binding international compute monitoring standard exists, and 118 countries remain outside any significant AI governance initiative.
open_problems.source: UN HLAB-AI, 'Governing AI for Humanity,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The UN process is inclusive (193 members) but has produced only aspirational frameworks. The first UN global AI forum isn't until July 2026. The real gap is between the US, EU, and China on who monitors what.

agendas.name: Chip Export Controls & Hardware Governance
agendas.description: Using semiconductor supply chain chokepoints to govern AI development — export controls, on-chip governance mechanisms, and hardware-enabled safety.

papers.title: Location Verification for AI Chips
papers.description: Introduces a design for a mechanism that can work on existing chips and verify their location to help enforce export control agreements.

papers.title: Hardware-Enabled Governance Mechanisms: Developing Technical Solutions to Govern AI Chip Usage
papers.description: Explores technical solutions for governing AI chip usage, including on-chip mechanisms for export control enforcement.

papers.title: Understanding the Artificial Intelligence Diffusion Framework
papers.description: Analyzes the January 2025 AI Diffusion Framework export control rule and its implications for US-led global AI governance.

papers.title: Evaluating An AI Chip Registration Policy
papers.description: Assesses semiconductor chip registration as a governance tool with practical policy recommendations.

papers.title: Explaining All the US Semiconductor Export Controls
papers.description: Comprehensive overview of US semiconductor export controls targeting China, explaining the strategic framework and evolution through multiple policy updates.

agendas.research_direction: Use semiconductor supply chain chokepoints to govern AI development. The AI chip supply chain is extraordinarily concentrated — a small number of companies (TSMC, ASML, NVIDIA) control critical nodes. This agenda explores how export controls, on-chip governance mechanisms, and hardware-level verification can serve as governance levers. Research spans from geopolitical analysis of chip export controls to technical proposals for tamper-proof chips that enforce usage policies.
agendas.theory_of_change: If you can control who gets advanced AI chips and verify how they're used, you can control who can train frontier AI models. This is a hardware-level chokepoint that is much harder to circumvent than software-level restrictions. The US has already demonstrated this lever with semiconductor export controls on China. Research aims to make these controls more precise, verifiable, and resistant to circumvention.
agendas.progress: The US has implemented multiple rounds of semiconductor export controls (October 2022, October 2023, [January 2025 AI Diffusion Framework](https://www.rand.org/pubs/perspectives/PEA3776-1.html)). [IAPS](https://www.iaps.ai) has proposed [location verification for AI chips](https://www.iaps.ai/research/location-verification-for-ai-chips). [RAND](https://www.rand.org) has explored [on-chip governance mechanisms](https://www.rand.org/pubs/working_papers/WRA3056-1.html). The January 2025 AI Diffusion Framework created a tiered global system for chip access. However, enforcement remains imperfect — smuggling, stockpiling, and workarounds are documented. On-chip governance mechanisms remain theoretical.

open_problems.problem: How to prevent chip smuggling and stockpiling that undermines export controls
open_problems.best_answer: [CNAS](https://www.cnas.org/publications/reports/technology-to-secure-the-ai-chip-supply-chain-a-primer) documents 10,000-140,000+ advanced GPUs likely smuggled into China in 2024, with at least eight networks exceeding $100M each. One shell company called 'Luxuriate Your Life' moved $390M in chips. They propose end-to-end chip tracking and mandatory seller reporting.
open_problems.source: CNAS, 'Technology to Secure the AI Chip Supply Chain,' 2024-2025
open_problems.critique: The scale of documented smuggling demonstrates enforcement failure. Chip companies' only incentive to prevent it is fear of fines, since smuggled chips still sell at full price.

open_problems.problem: Whether on-chip governance mechanisms (kill switches, usage tracking) are technically feasible without unacceptable performance costs
open_problems.best_answer: [Shavit et al.](https://www.cnas.org/publications/reports/secure-governable-chips) (CNAS, 2024) propose firmware-based licensing and on-chip verification using Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs), noting much functionality already exists in AMD, Apple, Intel, and NVIDIA chips. They argue this can work without meaningful performance degradation.
open_problems.source: Shavit et al., 'Secure, Governable Chips,' CNAS, 2024
open_problems.critique: The most technically grounded proposal, but faces objections: on-chip mechanisms could create new cybersecurity vulnerabilities, and the Data Innovation Foundation argues policymakers should reject kill switches entirely. No independent performance benchmarking has been published.

open_problems.problem: How to handle the transition to non-US chip manufacturing (e.g., Chinese domestic chip production)
open_problems.best_answer: [CSIS](https://www.csis.org/analysis/chinas-localization-drive-semiconductors-gains-impetus-allied-chip-export-controls) (2025) documents that China's domestic AI chip market share is projected to reach 50% by 2026. China mandates 50% domestic equipment for new fab capacity. SMIC has demonstrated multi-patterning DUV as an EUV alternative, and China has built an EUV prototype. Export controls have accelerated, not prevented, Chinese self-sufficiency.
open_problems.source: CSIS, 'China's Localization Drive in Semiconductors,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Convincingly demonstrates that controls have backfired on the self-sufficiency dimension. A quality gap likely remains at cutting edge (sub-7nm), but is closing. The core policy question — what to do when controls lose their bite — remains unanswered.

open_problems.problem: Balancing export controls against harms to allied nations and the global research community
open_problems.best_answer: [ITIF](https://itif.org/publications/2025/11/10/decoupling-risks-semiconductor-export-controls-harm-us-chipmakers-innovation/) estimates full decoupling could reduce US semiconductor R&D by ~24% ($14B) and cost 80,000+ jobs. But [CSIS](https://www.csis.org/analysis/did-us-semiconductor-export-controls-harm-innovation) found current controls haven't yet harmed companies' innovative capabilities — affected firms even outperformed comparable unaffected ones.
open_problems.source: ITIF, 'Decoupling Risks,' 2025; CSIS, 'Did Export Controls Harm Innovation?' 2025
open_problems.critique: Somewhat contradictory conclusions — manageable harm from current partial controls vs. severe consequences from full decoupling. Neither addresses impact on academic researchers in non-allied nations.

open_problems.problem: Whether controls on older-generation chips are needed as algorithmic efficiency improves
open_problems.best_answer: Epoch AI's 8-month halving rate implies frontier capabilities will be achievable on older hardware within a few years. BIS added 'node-agnostic' tool controls in 2024. But no comprehensive framework for legacy chip governance exists — a genuine literature gap.
open_problems.source: Epoch AI, 'Algorithmic Progress in Language Models,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Nobody has systematically analyzed which older chips become governance-relevant on what timeline. Compounded by the enormous installed base of older chips already deployed globally.

categories.name: International Governance
categories.description: Designing international institutions, treaties, and coordination mechanisms to manage global AI risks — analogies to nuclear governance, climate agreements, and biosecurity regimes.

agendas.name: International AI Treaties & Agreements
agendas.description: Proposals for binding international agreements on AI development, including moratoria, capability limits, and verification regimes.

papers.title: AI Governance to Avoid Extinction: The Strategic Landscape and Actionable Research Questions
papers.description: MIRI TGT's research agenda outlining four strategic scenarios for navigating advanced AI risks and arguing that building global Off Switch infrastructure is the most credible path to avoiding extinction.

papers.title: Mechanisms to Verify International Agreements About AI Development
papers.description: In-depth overview of mechanisms to verify adherence to international AI agreements, including on-chip mechanisms, physical inspections, and interconnect bandwidth limits.

papers.title: International Agreements on AI Safety: Review and Recommendations for a Conditional AI Safety Treaty
papers.description: Reviews recent proposals for international AI agreements and proposes a treaty establishing a compute threshold above which development requires rigorous oversight by AI Safety Institutes.

papers.title: International Institutions for Advanced AI
papers.description: Multi-author proposal for international institutional arrangements to govern advanced AI development.

papers.title: What Should Be Internationalised in AI Governance?
papers.venue: Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative / GovAI
papers.description: GovAI white paper examining trade-offs in international governance arrangements for civilian AI, investigating remedies to increase voice for the Global AI Majority.

papers.title: What AI Governance Can Learn From Climate — And Why It Mostly Hasn't
papers.description: Argues AI governance communities have failed to incorporate lessons from climate policy on systemic risks and international coordination, despite narrower windows for action.

papers.title: Verification Methods for International AI Agreements
papers.description: Catalogs 10 verification methods across three categories: national technical means, access-dependent, and hardware-dependent. Six independent layers make evasion difficult.

agendas.research_direction: Design binding international agreements that prevent dangerous AI development — the governance equivalent of nuclear nonproliferation treaties. Research spans from high-level strategic frameworks (what scenarios require what kinds of agreements) to detailed institutional design (verification mechanisms, enforcement provisions, compute thresholds). The most developed proposal is [MIRI TGT](https://techgov.intelligence.org)'s 'Off Switch' framework: building international infrastructure to halt frontier AI development if it becomes too dangerous.
agendas.theory_of_change: AI development is a global coordination problem. No single country's regulations are sufficient if other countries race ahead unsafely. International agreements can create mutual commitments that reduce competitive pressure to cut safety corners. The theory of change depends on the threat being severe enough that major powers prefer coordination to unilateral racing — analogous to how nuclear weapons' destructiveness motivated arms control despite deep US-Soviet rivalry.
agendas.progress: This is the most ambitious and least mature governance agenda. [MIRI TGT](https://techgov.intelligence.org)'s 2025 research agenda ([Barnett & Scher](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.04592)) provides the most structured strategic framework, organized around four geopolitical scenarios. [Scholefield et al. (2025)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.18956) proposed a conditional AI safety treaty with compute thresholds. [Ho et al. (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.04699) designed international institutional arrangements. The Bletchley-Seoul-Paris summit process has created diplomatic momentum but no binding commitments. The [International AI Safety Report](https://internationalaisafetyreport.org/publication/international-ai-safety-report-2026) (2026) represents the first global scientific consensus document.

open_problems.problem: How to verify compliance with AI development limits when training can be done in secret
open_problems.best_answer: [Wasil et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.16074) (2024) catalog 10 verification methods across three categories: national technical means (remote sensing, energy monitoring), access-dependent (on-site inspections), and hardware-dependent (chip tracking, on-chip mechanisms). A companion [RAND report](https://www.rand.org/pubs/working_papers/WRA4077-1.html) proposes six largely independent verification layers — evasion requires defeating all simultaneously.
open_problems.source: Wasil et al., 'Verification Methods for International AI Agreements,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The strongest work in this space. The layered approach is compelling, analogous to nuclear verification. But AI training produces no radioactive signatures, can happen in commercial data centers, and can be paused/resumed. The proposals require R&D investment and political will that don't yet exist.

open_problems.problem: Whether the US and China can agree on any binding AI constraints given current geopolitical tensions
open_problems.best_answer: [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/perspectives/PEA4189-1.html) identifies narrow areas: preventing AI control over nuclear decisions (already agreed in principle), biosecurity, and critical infrastructure protection. But neither country signed the military AI declaration at the most recent summit. Any hard law approach is assessed as 'out of reach.'
open_problems.source: RAND, 'Potential for U.S.-China Cooperation on Reducing AI Risks,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Realistic about how narrow the feasible cooperation space is. The nuclear AI agreement is significant but also the lowest-hanging fruit. Both nations view AI leadership as critical to national security, making comprehensive binding constraints extremely unlikely.

open_problems.problem: What compute or capability thresholds should trigger international restrictions
open_problems.best_answer: [MIRI TGT](https://techgov.intelligence.org/blog/considerations-for-setting-the-flop-thresholds-in-our-example-international-ai-agreement) (2025) provides the most detailed threshold analysis. The key insight from multiple researchers: nobody knows how many FLOP are needed to train dangerous AI systems, making threshold-setting fundamentally uncertain.
open_problems.source: MIRI TGT, 'Considerations for Setting FLOP Thresholds,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Well-articulated fundamental uncertainty. The EU threshold already captures many non-frontier models while the US threshold may miss efficient ones. No proposal adequately handles the interaction with algorithmic efficiency.

open_problems.problem: How to design agreements that are robust to rapid technological change
open_problems.best_answer: The [OECD AI Principles](https://oecd.ai/en/wonk/evolving-with-innovation-the-2024-oecd-ai-principles-update) (updated 2024) are designed as a flexible framework. The Global Digital Compact adds an International Scientific Panel (40 experts), a Global Dialogue on AI Governance for ongoing adjustment, and built-in review cycles. [CNAS](https://www.cnas.org/publications/reports/future-proofing-frontier-ai-regulation) proposes outcome-based rather than technology-specific requirements.
open_problems.source: OECD AI Principles, 2024; CNAS, 'Future-Proofing Frontier AI Regulation'
open_problems.critique: 'Flexible and adaptable' is easy to describe, hard to implement — flexibility often means toothlessness. By the time international institutions identify a problem and negotiate a response, the technology has moved on.

open_problems.problem: Whether a global moratorium on frontier AI is politically feasible, and what alternatives exist
open_problems.best_answer: [Convergence Analysis](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/6nNwMbdRXZDuNd4Gx/analysis-of-global-ai-governance-strategies) evaluates three strategies — Cooperative Development, Strategic Advantage, and Global Moratorium — finding the moratorium viable only under short timelines and hard alignment. The primary alternatives are compute-based governance that slows rather than stops development, and licensing regimes.
open_problems.source: Convergence Analysis, 'Analysis of Global AI Governance Strategies,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Correctly identifies that a moratorium is politically infeasible under current conditions. The alternatives are more feasible but much weaker. Missing: analysis of targeted moratoria on specific capability domains.

open_problems.problem: How to include non-state actors (AI labs) in international frameworks
open_problems.best_answer: The [Carnegie Endowment](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/03/envisioning-a-global-regime-complex-to-govern-artificial-intelligence) proposes a multi-layered 'regime complex' incorporating industry, civil society, and technical communities alongside states. The AI Safety Summit series includes lab CEOs alongside heads of state.
open_problems.source: Carnegie Endowment, 'Envisioning a Global Regime Complex,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Including labs as stakeholders in dialogues is fundamentally different from giving them binding obligations under international law. Multi-stakeholder models work for standard-setting but have a poor track record for enforcement.

agendas.name: AI Safety Summits & Multilateral Processes
agendas.description: The Bletchley-Seoul-Paris summit process, the International AI Safety Report, and multilateral consensus-building.

papers.title: The AI Governance Arms Race: From Summit Pageantry to Progress?
papers.description: Critical analysis of the AI safety summit process from Bletchley to Seoul, assessing whether summit pageantry translates into real governance progress.

papers.title: The State of Implementation of the OECD AI Principles Four Years On
papers.description: Assesses how OECD member countries have implemented the OECD AI Principles four years after their adoption.

agendas.research_direction: Analyze and improve the multilateral summit process for AI governance — the Bletchley Park (2023) → Seoul (2024) → Paris (2025) → Delhi (2026) series and associated institutions. Research evaluates whether summits produce real governance progress or merely diplomatic theater, and how to make multilateral processes more effective at addressing catastrophic risk.
agendas.theory_of_change: Summits create political momentum, build relationships between national leaders, and establish norms. The [Bletchley Declaration](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ai-safety-summit-2023-the-bletchley-declaration) was the first time major AI nations (including China) jointly acknowledged catastrophic risk. If summits can sustain political attention and ratchet up commitments over time — as climate COPs did — they can lay the groundwork for binding agreements.
agendas.progress: The summit process has produced the [Bletchley Declaration](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/ai-safety-summit-2023-the-bletchley-declaration), [Seoul Frontier AI Safety Commitments](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/frontier-ai-safety-commitments-ai-seoul-summit-2024) (signed by 16 companies), and the [International AI Safety Report](https://internationalaisafetyreport.org/publication/international-ai-safety-report-2026) (2026). National AI Safety Institutes were established in the UK and US partly due to summit momentum. However, critics argue the process has been heavy on pageantry and light on enforceable commitments. China's engagement has been inconsistent.

open_problems.problem: How to convert voluntary commitments into binding obligations
open_problems.best_answer: The [EU AI Act's Code of Practice](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/introduction-to-code-of-practice/) is the most developed mechanism: technically voluntary but with strong incentives — non-participants face stricter scrutiny and must independently demonstrate compliance. Non-compliance after August 2026 carries fines up to 15M EUR or 3% of global turnover.
open_problems.source: EU AI Act Code of Practice for GPAI, 2025
open_problems.critique: Genuinely innovative 'voluntold' approach. But applies only within EU jurisdiction. Enforcement requires an estimated 3x increase in AI Office staff. Doesn't address global voluntary commitments like the Bletchley Declaration.

open_problems.problem: Whether the summit process can maintain momentum as AI governance becomes more politically contested
open_problems.best_answer: The [European Policy Centre](https://www.epc.eu/publication/The-Paris-Summit-Au-Revoir-global-AI-Safety-61ea68/) (2025) documents the cracking: the US and UK refused to sign the Paris declaration, objecting to references to existential risk, environmental impact, and UN involvement. The summit series shifted from 'safety' (Bletchley 2023) to 'action' (Paris 2025) to 'impact' (India 2026), reflecting dilution of the safety focus.
open_problems.source: EPC, 'The Paris Summit: Au Revoir, Global AI Safety?' 2025
open_problems.critique: Accurately captures the loss of momentum. The process is not anchored in any institution, making it vulnerable to political shifts. Anthropic's CEO called Paris a 'missed opportunity.'

open_problems.problem: How to ensure Global South representation and voice in what has been a US/UK-led process
open_problems.best_answer: The India AI Impact Summit (2026) is the first in the Global South. But 118 countries remain outside any significant AI governance initiative. The [UNCTAD Technology and Innovation Report 2025](https://unctad.org/publication/technology-and-innovation-report-2025) provides the most comprehensive Global South perspective, but no convincing mechanism for genuine power-sharing has been proposed.
open_problems.source: UNCTAD, 'Technology and Innovation Report 2025'
open_problems.critique: Moving summits to the Global South is symbolic but doesn't address structural power asymmetries — labs, compute, and expertise remain concentrated in the US and China.

open_problems.problem: Whether AI-specific summits or integration into existing institutions (G7, UN) is more effective
open_problems.best_answer: [Carnegie Endowment](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/03/envisioning-a-global-regime-complex-to-govern-artificial-intelligence) argues for a 'regime complex' — parallel tracks through AI summits, UN, G7, and regional bodies are actually beneficial despite appearing fragmented. Different institutions serve different functions (standards via ISO, norms via summits, enforcement via EU, inclusion via UN).
open_problems.source: Carnegie Endowment, 'Envisioning a Global Regime Complex,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Intellectually sound but the downside is exactly what Paris demonstrated: no single institution can compel action. Each institution can be played off against others.

agendas.name: Arms Control Analogies
agendas.description: Applying lessons from nuclear arms control, biological weapons conventions, and other international security regimes to AI governance.

papers.title: Historical Analogues That Can Inform AI Governance
papers.description: Examines governance approaches for nuclear technology, the internet, encryption, and genetic engineering to extract lessons for AI governance.

agendas.research_direction: Extract governance lessons from historical regimes for controlling dangerous technologies — nuclear weapons, biological weapons, chemical weapons, internet governance, encryption policy. Research identifies which features of past governance successes and failures transfer to AI, and which don't. Key question: is AI more like nuclear weapons (concentrated, detectable, state-controlled) or bioweapons (diffuse, dual-use, hard to verify)?
agendas.theory_of_change: We don't have to design AI governance from scratch. Past technology governance regimes offer tested institutional designs, verification mechanisms, and negotiation strategies. By understanding why nuclear nonproliferation mostly worked while bioweapons verification mostly failed, we can design better AI governance institutions.
agendas.progress: [RAND](https://www.rand.org)'s 2024 report systematically compared AI governance to nuclear, internet, encryption, and genetic engineering governance. The compute-monitoring community explicitly draws on nuclear safeguards analogies. However, the field is still debating which analogies are most apt — the AI governance problem has unique features (speed of development, dual-use nature, private-sector dominance) that limit direct transfer.

open_problems.problem: Whether any historical analogy adequately captures AI's combination of speed, dual-use nature, and private-sector development
open_problems.best_answer: [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA3408-1.html) (2024) systematically compares AI to nuclear, internet, encryption, and genetic engineering governance. The key finding is that no single analogy is adequate — AI combines nuclear's destructive potential, bioweapons' dual-use nature, the internet's speed of proliferation, and private-sector development patterns that don't match any historical precedent.
open_problems.source: RAND, 'Historical Analogues That Can Inform AI Governance,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Honest about the limits of analogical reasoning. But concluding 'no single analogy works' leaves governance designers without a template. The question is whether combining elements from multiple analogies can work, and this remains unexplored.

open_problems.problem: How to adapt verification regimes designed for physical materials to software and compute
open_problems.best_answer: [Wasil et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.16074) (2024) provide the most systematic treatment, cataloging 10 verification methods. The [IAEA-for-AI proposal](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03017-1) (Nature, 2024) draws explicit lessons from nuclear safeguards. But AI training produces no physical signatures equivalent to radioactive material, fundamentally limiting adaptability.
open_problems.source: Wasil et al., 'Verification Methods for International AI Agreements,' 2024; Trager et al., 'Lessons from the IAEA,' Nature, 2024
open_problems.critique: The strongest available work. The layered approach is compelling but requires political will and R&D investment that don't exist. The absence of physical signatures is a fundamental barrier, not just a technical challenge.

open_problems.problem: Whether AI governance requires entirely new institutional forms rather than adaptations of existing ones
open_problems.best_answer: [Carnegie](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/03/envisioning-a-global-regime-complex-to-govern-artificial-intelligence) (2024) argues for a 'regime complex' — not a single new institution but a web of existing and new institutions working in concert. This is the emerging consensus among governance scholars, though MIRI TGT's 'Off Switch' proposal represents the most radical institutional innovation (a global monitoring and shutdown capability with no historical precedent).
open_problems.source: Carnegie, 'Global Regime Complex,' 2024; MIRI TGT research agenda, 2025
open_problems.critique: The regime complex approach is pragmatic but may be insufficient for catastrophic risks that require coordinated rapid response. MIRI TGT's proposal is the only truly novel institutional form but also the most politically infeasible. The field is stuck between pragmatic incrementalism and necessary radicalism.

categories.name: Corporate Governance
categories.description: Internal governance structures at AI labs — responsible scaling policies, safety cases, deployment decisions, board governance, and whistleblower protections.

agendas.name: Responsible Scaling Policies & Safety Frameworks
agendas.description: Commitments by frontier AI labs to tie capability increases to safety milestones — Anthropic's RSP, OpenAI's Preparedness Framework, DeepMind's Frontier Safety Framework.

papers.title: Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy (v1.0)
papers.description: First version of Anthropic's RSP introducing AI Safety Levels (ASL-1 through ASL-4), modeled on biosafety levels, tying capability increases to safety milestones.

papers.title: OpenAI Preparedness Framework (Beta)
papers.description: OpenAI's framework for tracking and preparing for advanced AI capabilities in biological/chemical, cybersecurity, and AI self-improvement risk categories.

papers.title: Introducing the Frontier Safety Framework
papers.description: DeepMind's Frontier Safety Framework defining Critical Capability Levels (CCLs) and corresponding safety mitigations, led by Anca Dragan, Helen King, and Allan Dafoe.

papers.title: Common Elements of Frontier AI Safety Policies
papers.description: Comprehensive comparison of frontier AI safety policies across 12 companies, analyzing common elements like capability thresholds, model weight security, and deployment mitigations.

papers.title: Frontier AI Safety Commitments (Seoul Summit)
papers.description: Sixteen AI companies agreed to voluntary frontier AI safety commitments at the AI Seoul Summit in May 2024.

papers.title: All the Labs' AI Safety Plans: 2025 Edition
papers.description: Comprehensive comparison of safety plans from all major AI labs including their governance structures, RSPs, and evaluation commitments.

papers.title: Companies' Safety Plans Neglect Risks from Scheming AI
papers.description: Argues that current lab safety plans systematically underweight the risk of deceptive/scheming AI behavior, a critical gap in corporate governance frameworks.

agendas.research_direction: Evaluate and improve the commitments frontier AI labs make to tie capability increases to safety milestones. [Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy](https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropics-responsible-scaling-policy) (ASL levels), [OpenAI's Preparedness Framework](https://cdn.openai.com/openai-preparedness-framework-beta.pdf), and [DeepMind's Frontier Safety Framework](https://deepmind.google/discover/blog/introducing-the-frontier-safety-framework/) are the primary objects of study. Research compares these frameworks, identifies gaps, and asks whether voluntary corporate commitments can be trusted.
agendas.theory_of_change: If labs credibly commit to not scaling capabilities beyond what their safety measures can handle, the most dangerous training runs won't happen. RSPs create internal tripwires that, if honored, prevent the worst outcomes. The theory depends on labs actually following their commitments — which is why external verification ([METR](https://metr.org), AISIs) and public accountability matter.
agendas.progress: All major frontier labs now have published safety frameworks (as of 2025). [METR](https://metr.org)'s ['Common Elements' comparison](https://metr.org/common-elements) provides the first comprehensive cross-lab analysis. The Seoul Summit commitments added a layer of international peer pressure. However, [Apollo Research](https://www.apolloresearch.ai) has shown that these plans systematically neglect scheming risks. Labs have been criticized for setting thresholds they haven't yet approached, making it unclear whether commitments will hold under commercial pressure.

open_problems.problem: Whether labs will actually pause or slow development when they hit their stated thresholds
open_problems.best_answer: The [FLI AI Safety Index](https://futureoflife.org/ai-safety-index-winter-2025/) (Winter 2025) finds no lab scored above C+; all received D or below on existential safety. 'Rhetoric about addressing existential risks has not translated into quantitative safety plans.' Anthropic's own RSP v3.0 admitted 'pre-defined capability thresholds have proven harder to interpret than expected.'
open_problems.source: FLI AI Safety Index Winter 2025; Anthropic RSP v3.0, February 2026
open_problems.critique: Anthropic's candid admission is the most informative data point — even the most safety-committed lab finds it hard to define when to pause. The commitment remains untested under real competitive pressure.

open_problems.problem: How to verify that internal safety evaluations are conducted rigorously and honestly
open_problems.best_answer: Anthropic's RSP v3.0 introduces external review of Risk Reports 'in certain circumstances' and publishes them online every 3-6 months (with redactions). [UK AISI](https://www.aisi.gov.uk/blog/5-key-findings-from-our-first-frontier-ai-trends-report) and US AISI conduct some independent evaluations. But no mandatory independent audit regime exists.
open_problems.source: Anthropic RSP v3.0, 2026; UK AISI, 'Frontier AI Trends Report,' 2025
open_problems.critique: External review is a step forward but remains at the lab's discretion. Redactions undermine verification value. No equivalent of financial auditing standards (independence, materiality, professional liability) exists for AI safety.

open_problems.problem: Whether RSPs adequately address risks from scheming/deceptive AI behavior
open_problems.best_answer: Anthropic's '[Three Sketches of ASL-4 Safety Case Components](https://alignment.anthropic.com/2024/safety-cases/)' (2024) requires demonstrating evaluation procedures 'are robust to an AI motivated to undermine them.' The [Alignment Forum critique](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/9nEBWxjAHSu3ncr6v/responsible-scaling-policies-are-risk-management-done-wrong) argues RSPs use underspecified risk definitions and fail to quantify risk. Conservative estimates: RSPs reduce catastrophic risk by only 10-25%.
open_problems.source: Anthropic, 'Three Sketches of ASL-4 Safety Case Components,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The core circularity is acknowledged but unresolved: you need to trust evaluations, but a scheming model's priority would be undermining evaluations. No RSP has a credible plan for detecting sophisticated deception.

open_problems.problem: How to handle the gap between voluntary commitments and legally enforceable requirements
open_problems.best_answer: The EU AI Act's Code of Practice is the leading conversion mechanism (see Summits). In the US, [27 AI laws were enacted across 14 states](https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48555) from 2023-2025, creating a patchwork. The gap is most acute for catastrophic risks — no jurisdiction has binding requirements for frontier safety evaluations, pause capabilities, or alignment research investment.
open_problems.source: CRS, 'Regulating AI: U.S. and International Approaches,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The EU leads on voluntary-to-binding conversion but enforcement capacity is a serious concern. The US lacks federal AI safety legislation entirely.

open_problems.problem: Whether safety frameworks need to account for cumulative risk across multiple model releases
open_problems.best_answer: '[Measurement Challenges in AI Catastrophic Risk Governance](https://arxiv.org/html/2410.00608v1)' (2024) argues cumulative automation across domains could lead to catastrophic risks even if no single model is dangerous alone. Proposes 'rigorous model interaction simulators.' But every existing RSP evaluates models individually — this is arguably the most under-addressed problem in the entire RSP framework.
open_problems.source: 'Measurement Challenges in AI Catastrophic Risk Governance,' arXiv, 2024
open_problems.critique: The problem is correctly identified but barely addressed in practice. No lab has a framework for assessing how a new release changes overall risk when combined with existing and open-source models. The proposed simulators don't exist.

agendas.name: Lab Governance & Risk Management
agendas.description: How AI labs structure decision-making authority, safety review boards, and internal risk governance.

papers.title: Towards Best Practices in AGI Safety and Governance: A Survey of Expert Opinion
papers.description: Survey of 92 leading experts identifying areas of consensus on best practices for AGI safety and governance at AI labs.

papers.title: Transforming Risk Governance at Frontier AI Companies
papers.description: Explores how the Three Lines Model (3LoD) for risk governance could be implemented at frontier AI companies to ensure safer model development.

papers.title: Why Frontier AI Safety Frameworks Need to Include Risk Governance
papers.description: Argues that frontier AI safety frameworks must incorporate systematic risk governance, not just technical safety measures.

papers.title: PAI's Guidance for Safe Foundation Model Deployment
papers.description: Framework for responsible foundation model deployment developed through a participatory process with over 40 institutions.

papers.title: Amoral Drift in AI Corporate Governance
papers.description: Documents how equity-based compensation creates 'superstakeholders' whose financial interests overwhelm mission-driven governance. The OpenAI case study is the definitive example.

agendas.research_direction: Design internal governance structures at AI labs — safety review boards, decision-making authority, risk management frameworks, and organizational checks on deployment decisions. Research asks how labs should be organized to make good decisions under uncertainty about catastrophic risks, drawing on risk governance practices from nuclear, pharmaceutical, and financial industries.
agendas.theory_of_change: The decision to train or deploy a potentially dangerous AI system is ultimately made by people inside labs. If internal governance structures ensure that safety concerns can override commercial pressures — through independent safety boards, clear escalation paths, and whistleblower protections — the probability of catastrophic deployment decisions decreases.
agendas.progress: [GovAI](https://www.governance.ai)'s [expert survey (Schuett et al., 2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.07153) identified areas of consensus on best practices. [CLTR](https://www.longtermresilience.org) has adapted the [Three Lines Model](https://www.longtermresilience.org/reports/transforming-risk-governance-at-frontier-ai-companies/) from financial risk governance to frontier AI labs. Some labs have created internal safety review boards. However, the OpenAI board crisis (November 2023) demonstrated that even well-designed governance structures can fail under commercial pressure, and that boards may lack the power to override CEOs on safety grounds.

open_problems.problem: How to design boards that can actually overrule commercial incentives on safety grounds
open_problems.best_answer: '[Amoral Drift in AI Corporate Governance](https://harvardlawreview.org/print/vol-138/amoral-drift-in-ai-corporate-governance/)' (Harvard Law Review, 2025) documents how OpenAI's governance failed: employees whose equity had increased ~300% threatened mass departure, and safety directors were replaced. Anthropic's response — Class T shareholders controlling 3 of 5 board seats after May 2027 — is the most promising structural innovation.
open_problems.source: 'Amoral Drift in AI Corporate Governance,' Harvard Law Review Vol. 138, 2025
open_problems.critique: The best available analysis. OpenAI is the only real-world test, and safety lost decisively. Anthropic's Class T structure is untested and depends on trustees willing to exercise power against commercial pressure — the same problem one level up.

open_problems.problem: Whether the Three Lines Model (or any existing risk framework) adequately captures AI-specific risks
open_problems.best_answer: [GovAI](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-023-01811-0) adapted 3LoD for frontier AI labs (AI & Society, 2023). The [MIT AI Risk Repository](https://airisk.mit.edu/blog/mapping-frameworks-at-the-intersection-of-ai-safety-and-traditional-risk-management) maps frameworks at the AI safety/risk management intersection. Both find 3LoD adaptable but note AI-specific risks (deceptive alignment, emergent capabilities) require specialized expertise standard risk teams lack.
open_problems.source: GovAI, 'Three Lines of Defense Against Risks from AI,' AI & Society, 2023
open_problems.critique: Reasonable adaptation but the fundamental limitation is that 3LoD assumes risks are identifiable and measurable. Frontier AI risks are characterized by deep uncertainty about what capabilities will emerge and when. No framework designed for known-unknowns has been adapted for unknown-unknowns.

open_problems.problem: How to retain institutional knowledge and safety culture through rapid lab growth and staff turnover
open_problems.best_answer: No dedicated research paper addresses this in the AI lab context. The [FLI AI Safety Index](https://futureoflife.org/ai-safety-index-winter-2025/) evaluates governance but not knowledge retention. High-reliability organization theory (nuclear, aviation) is relevant but has not been systematically applied to AI labs. The departure of safety staff from multiple labs has received media but no academic treatment.
open_problems.source: FLI AI Safety Index, 2025
open_problems.critique: A genuine blind spot. AI labs are among the fastest-growing organizations in history, yet there is no published research on how safety culture is maintained or lost during this growth.

open_problems.problem: What legal structures (benefit corporations, trusts, etc.) best align lab incentives with safety
open_problems.best_answer: The [Harvard Law Review](https://harvardlawreview.org/print/vol-138/amoral-drift-in-ai-corporate-governance/) 'Amoral Drift' article (2025) systematically compares Public Benefit Corporation status, capped-profit models, and trust-based governance. It shows equity-based compensation creates 'superstakeholders' whose financial interests overwhelm mission-driven governance regardless of formal structure. OpenAI's conversion from nonprofit to effectively for-profit is the definitive case study.
open_problems.source: 'Amoral Drift in AI Corporate Governance,' Harvard Law Review, 2025
open_problems.critique: Compelling — every legal structure tried so far has been vulnerable to commercial pressure. The missing question: whether any corporate form can withstand multi-trillion-dollar market incentives, or whether only external regulation can provide adequate constraints.

categories.name: Regulatory Frameworks
categories.description: National and regional regulatory approaches to AI — the EU AI Act, US executive orders, UK pro-innovation framework, and licensing proposals.

agendas.name: AI Acts & Legislation
agendas.description: Major legislative efforts to regulate AI systems, particularly those posing catastrophic risks.

papers.title: Executive Order on the Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence
papers.description: Biden administration executive order establishing reporting requirements for frontier AI training, red-teaming mandates, and NIST standards development.

papers.title: 2024 State of the AI Regulatory Landscape
papers.description: First-of-its-kind comparative overview of AI regulatory approaches in the US, EU, and China as of May 2024.

papers.title: Regulating AI in the UK
papers.description: Analysis of the UK Government's AI regulatory approach, identifying significant gaps in the pro-innovation framework.

papers.title: SB-1047 Safe and Secure Innovation for Frontier Artificial Intelligence Models Act
papers.description: California bill requiring safety testing for frontier AI models above compute thresholds, vetoed by Governor Newsom but influential in shaping the national debate on AI regulation.

agendas.research_direction: Analyze and design national and regional legislation for AI governance. The [EU AI Act](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/), US Executive Orders, UK's pro-innovation framework, California's [SB-1047](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billNavClient.xhtml?bill_id=202320240SB1047) (vetoed), and China's AI regulations are the primary objects of study. Research compares approaches, identifies gaps, and proposes improvements specifically targeting catastrophic risk.
agendas.theory_of_change: Legislation creates legally binding obligations with enforcement mechanisms. Unlike voluntary commitments, laws apply regardless of whether companies want to comply. The right legislation can mandate safety testing, require incident reporting, establish liability, and create regulators with real power. The wrong legislation can be either toothless or counterproductive.
agendas.progress: The [EU AI Act](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/) (2024) is the most comprehensive AI legislation globally, with specific provisions for 'general-purpose AI' including frontier models. The [Biden Executive Order](https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/presidential-actions/2023/10/30/executive-order-on-the-safe-secure-and-trustworthy-development-and-use-of-artificial-intelligence/) (2023) established reporting requirements and safety testing mandates for models above 10^26 FLOP. [SB-1047](https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billNavClient.xhtml?bill_id=202320240SB1047) in California would have been the most stringent US state law but was vetoed — yet it significantly shaped the national debate. China has implemented AI-specific regulations including algorithmic recommendation rules and generative AI rules.

open_problems.problem: Whether the EU AI Act's provisions for general-purpose AI are sufficient for catastrophic risk
open_problems.best_answer: The EU AI Act's static 10^25 FLOP threshold for 'systemic risk' classification will rapidly become inadequate. [Paul Scharre at CNAS](https://www.cnas.org/publications/reports/future-proofing-frontier-ai-regulation) projects effective compute could reach ~1 million times GPT-4 levels by the early 2030s, meaning the threshold captures either too many or too few models depending on direction of error.
open_problems.source: Paul Scharre, 'Future-Proofing Frontier AI Regulation,' CNAS, 2024
open_problems.critique: Strong on the threshold problem but doesn't evaluate whether the Act's qualitative mechanisms (adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity under Article 55) are adequate for truly catastrophic risk scenarios. The governance machinery beyond thresholds remains underexamined.

open_problems.problem: How US federal legislation will develop given partisan divisions on AI regulation
open_problems.best_answer: The Trump Administration's March 2026 'National Policy Framework for AI' calls for unified federal standards with state preemption. AI-related lobbyists in the US increased 120% from 2022-2023. The razor-thin GOP House majority and a 99-1 Senate vote against state preemption in 2025 suggest deep partisan fractures on the path forward.
open_problems.source: Gibson Dunn, 'Toward a National AI Policy?' March 2026
open_problems.critique: A snapshot of a rapidly evolving situation. Documents the dynamics well but the fundamental tension between federal preemption and state innovation remains genuinely unresolved.

open_problems.problem: Whether compute-based thresholds in legislation will remain meaningful as efficiency improves
open_problems.best_answer: [Epoch AI](https://epoch.ai/blog/algorithmic-progress-in-language-models) demonstrates that compute needed for a given capability level halves roughly every 8 months (95% CI: 5-14 months), far outpacing Moore's Law. A fixed FLOP threshold will capture exponentially more models over time while failing to capture efficient models achieving frontier capabilities below it. The [Institute for Law & AI](https://arxiv.org/html/2405.10799v2) recommends supplementing compute thresholds with capability-based assessments.
open_problems.source: Ho, Besiroglu et al., 'Algorithmic Progress in Language Models,' Epoch AI, 2024
open_problems.critique: The most empirically rigorous treatment. The 8-month halving rate is well-evidenced across 231 models. But no paper has proposed a fully satisfactory replacement — capability assessments are recommended but hard to standardize and game-resistant. The inference-compute paradigm (o1-style reasoning) further complicates training-compute-only thresholds.

open_problems.problem: How to regulate AI without stifling beneficial development or pushing it offshore
open_problems.best_answer: The [Carnegie Endowment](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/07/beyond-open-vs-closed-emerging-consensus-and-key-questions-for-foundation-ai-model-governance) argues against binary open/closed regulatory frameworks in favor of a spectrum of access controls calibrated to risk level, preventing the false choice between over-regulation and no regulation.
open_problems.source: Klyman, 'Beyond Open vs. Closed,' Carnegie Endowment, 2024
open_problems.critique: Conceptually sound but under-specified operationally. No paper has convincingly demonstrated how to set the gradient of regulation at the right level. The offshoring risk is acknowledged but the proposed solution (international harmonization) faces the same collective action problems it aims to solve.

open_problems.problem: How to update legislation fast enough to keep pace with AI capabilities
open_problems.best_answer: [Reuel & Undheim](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.04554) define and operationalize adaptive governance for AI, arguing that traditional rigid regulatory regimes are structurally unable to keep pace. They propose a framework with actors, roles, and shared policy activities that can evolve alongside technology. The UK's deliberately non-statutory approach is the closest real-world experiment.
open_problems.source: Reuel & Undheim, 'Generative AI Needs Adaptive Governance,' arXiv, 2024
open_problems.critique: Well-articulated framework but acknowledges its own risks: insufficient oversight depth, regulatory uncertainty, and vulnerability to capture. No jurisdiction has fully implemented adaptive governance for AI — this remains theoretical. The UK's approach is closest but lacks enforcement teeth.

agendas.name: Frontier AI Regulation & Licensing
agendas.description: Proposals to require licenses for training or deploying frontier AI systems above certain capability thresholds.

papers.title: Frontier AI Regulation: Managing Emerging Risks to Public Safety
papers.description: Proposes a regulatory framework for frontier AI models based on capability thresholds, with graduated requirements for safety and reporting.

papers.title: From Principles to Rules: A Regulatory Approach for Frontier AI
papers.venue: arXiv / Oxford Handbook on Foundations and Regulation of Generative AI
papers.description: Examines principle-based vs. rule-based regulation for frontier AI, analyzing strengths and weaknesses of each approach.

papers.title: AI Governance at the Frontier
papers.description: Analytic approach to help US policymakers deconstruct AI governance proposals by identifying their underlying assumptions.

papers.title: Enabling Principles for AI Governance
papers.description: Offers three principles for US policymakers: tracking AI incidents, developing AI literacy, and preserving policy adaptability.

papers.title: The World's First Frontier AI Regulation Is Surprisingly Thoughtful: The EU's Code of Practice
papers.description: Analysis of the EU's GPAI Code of Practice as the first legally enforceable safety framework for frontier AI, requiring threat modeling, independent evaluations, and incident reporting.

papers.title: The AI Regulator's Toolbox: A List of Concrete AI Governance Interventions
papers.description: Enumerates concrete governance interventions available to regulators, from licensing and reporting requirements to compute thresholds and safety standards.

papers.title: How Do AI Companies 'Fine-Tune' Policy? Examining Regulatory Capture in AI Governance
papers.description: Empirical study based on 17 expert interviews documenting AI regulatory capture mechanisms. Found AI-related lobbyists in the US increased 120% from 2022-2023, with 85% hired by industry.

papers.title: Training Compute Thresholds: Features and Functions in AI Regulation
papers.description: Most systematic comparison of input-based vs. output-based thresholds for AI regulation. Recommends a hybrid approach using compute as screening trigger and capabilities as substantive assessment.

agendas.research_direction: Design licensing and registration regimes specifically for frontier AI development. Research proposes that training or deploying AI models above certain capability thresholds should require a license — analogous to nuclear facilities, pharmaceutical development, or broadcasting. Work covers threshold design, licensing conditions, enforcement mechanisms, and institutional capacity.
agendas.theory_of_change: Licensing creates a gatekeeping function: only developers who demonstrate adequate safety measures can proceed. It shifts the burden of proof from 'prove it's dangerous' to 'prove it's safe enough.' Licensing also creates a regulatory relationship that enables ongoing oversight, information sharing, and enforcement.
agendas.progress: [Anderljung et al. (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.03718) provided the most influential proposal for frontier AI regulation with graduated requirements. [Schuett et al. (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07300) analyzed principle-based vs. rule-based approaches. The [EU AI Act](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/)'s G[PAI](https://partnershiponai.org) Code of Practice (analyzed by [Kodama, 2025](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/Z4DYcBDd36mwr5Xpq/the-world-s-first-frontier-ai-regulation-is-surprisingly)) represents the first legally enforceable frontier AI safety framework. [CSET](https://cset.georgetown.edu) has produced analytic frameworks to help policymakers evaluate governance proposals. However, no country has yet implemented a full licensing regime for frontier AI development.

open_problems.problem: What capability or compute thresholds should trigger licensing requirements
open_problems.best_answer: The emerging consensus is that no single threshold is adequate. The [Carnegie Endowment](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2025/07/artificial-intelligence-regulation-united-states) argues for entity-based regulation — regulating organizations rather than models, based on cumulative risk profile rather than any single metric. [Epoch AI](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.16138v1) forecasts 103-306 models exceeding 10^25 FLOPs by 2028, showing how quickly any fixed threshold becomes meaningless.
open_problems.source: Carnegie Endowment, 'Entity-Based Regulation in Frontier AI Governance,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Entity-based regulation sidesteps the threshold obsolescence problem but introduces new issues: defining which entities qualify and creating barriers to entry for smaller developers.

open_problems.problem: How to build regulatory capacity fast enough — there are very few people who understand both AI and regulation
open_problems.best_answer: The [IAPP AI Governance Profession Report 2025](https://iapp.org/resources/article/ai-governance-profession-report) provides the most rigorous data: only 1.5% of surveyed organizations believe they have adequate governance headcount, and 23.5% cite lack of qualified professionals as a top barrier.
open_problems.source: IAPP, 'AI Governance Profession Report 2025'
open_problems.critique: Documents the problem well but offers largely aspirational solutions. Neither this nor the UN's capacity-building proposals address the core bottleneck: the tiny number of people who simultaneously understand ML at a technical level and have regulatory expertise.

open_problems.problem: Whether licensing should be based on inputs (compute, data) or outputs (capabilities, risks)
open_problems.best_answer: The [Institute for Law & AI](https://arxiv.org/html/2405.10799v2) provides the most systematic comparison: input-based thresholds (compute) are easier to measure but increasingly poor risk proxies, while output-based (capabilities) are more meaningful but harder to standardize. They recommend a hybrid: compute as a screening trigger, capabilities as the substantive assessment. This is increasingly reflected in policy (e.g., California SB 53).
open_problems.source: 'Training Compute Thresholds: Features and Functions in AI Regulation,' Institute for Law & AI, 2024
open_problems.critique: The most pragmatic answer currently available. Weakness: it defers the hardest question — how to evaluate capabilities reliably — to downstream processes that don't yet have robust methods.

open_problems.problem: How to prevent regulatory capture by the companies being regulated
open_problems.best_answer: [Wei et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.13042) document specific capture mechanisms via interviews with 17 AI policy experts: AI-related lobbyists in the US increased 120% from 2022-2023, with 85% hired by industry. They propose countermeasures including diversifying expert input, transparency requirements, and structural separation between industry participation and rule-making.
open_problems.source: Wei, Ezell, Gabrieli & Deshpande, 'How Do AI Companies Fine-Tune Policy?' AAAI/ACM AIES, 2024
open_problems.critique: The strongest empirical work on AI regulatory capture. The 120% lobbying increase is striking. But the proposed countermeasures are generic anti-capture measures. The deeper problem — regulators genuinely need industry input because the knowledge gap is enormous — makes some degree of capture structurally difficult to avoid.

open_problems.problem: How to design licensing that works across jurisdictions
open_problems.best_answer: The Cambridge Forum on AI proposes a mutual recognition framework where AI Safety Institutes conduct evaluations based on international standards and mutually recognize outcomes, drawing on precedent from pharmaceutical regulation.
open_problems.source: 'From Principles to Practice: Coordinated International LLMs Supervision,' Cambridge Forum on AI, 2025
open_problems.critique: Mutual recognition works in pharmaceuticals but faces unique challenges in AI where jurisdictions have fundamentally different values regarding acceptable risk, surveillance, and free expression. The US, EU, and China have divergent regulatory philosophies that mutual recognition cannot bridge.

categories.name: Misuse Prevention
categories.description: Preventing catastrophic misuse of AI systems — bioweapons, cyberattacks, autonomous weapons, and other WMD-relevant capabilities.

agendas.name: Biological & Chemical Weapons Risk
agendas.description: Assessing and mitigating the risk that AI systems could meaningfully assist in the creation of biological or chemical weapons.

papers.title: The Operational Risks of AI in Large-Scale Biological Attacks: A Red-Team Approach
papers.description: Methodology report for RAND's red-team study of whether LLMs could provide meaningful assistance for biological weapons attacks.

papers.title: The Operational Risks of AI in Large-Scale Biological Attacks: Results of a Red-Team Study
papers.description: Results of RAND's red-team study finding that current AI does not meaningfully increase risk of a biological weapons attack beyond existing resources.

papers.title: Mitigating Risks at the Intersection of Artificial Intelligence and Chemical and Biological Weapons
papers.description: Assesses AI risks related to chemical and biological threats and how AI misuse could be mitigated or prevented.

papers.title: AI and Chemical & Biological Weapons
papers.description: FLI position paper on the risks of AI enabling chemical and biological weapons development.

papers.title: Managing Catastrophic Misuse Without Robust AIs
papers.description: Proposes governance strategies for preventing catastrophic misuse in a world where AI systems are not yet robust enough to self-police, focusing on structural safeguards.

papers.title: Safetywashing: Do AI Safety Benchmarks Actually Measure Safety Progress?
papers.description: Demonstrates that most AI safety benchmarks — including biosafety ones — are highly correlated with general capabilities rather than measuring a distinct safety dimension, meaning capability gains can be misrepresented as safety progress.

papers.title: Deep Ignorance: Filtering Pretraining Data Builds Tamper-Resistant Safeguards into Open-Weight LLMs
papers.description: Proposes filtering dangerous knowledge from pretraining data, creating safeguards 10x more resistant to adversarial fine-tuning than post-hoc methods.

agendas.research_direction: Assess and mitigate the risk that AI systems could meaningfully assist in creating biological or chemical weapons. Research uses red-teaming methodologies to evaluate whether current and near-future AI models provide 'uplift' — meaningful assistance beyond what's available through textbooks and the internet. Work also designs safeguards, filters, and governance mechanisms specific to CBRN risks.
agendas.theory_of_change: If AI models can provide step-by-step guidance for synthesizing dangerous pathogens or chemical weapons, the barrier to bioterrorism drops dramatically. Governance interventions — pre-deployment testing for CBRN knowledge, targeted output filters, structured access for dual-use models — can reduce this risk without blocking beneficial uses of AI in biology and chemistry.
agendas.progress: [RAND](https://www.rand.org)'s red-team studies (Mouton et al., 2023-2024) found that current LLMs do not yet meaningfully increase biological attack risk beyond existing resources. However, multiple AI companies in 2025 added extra safeguards to new model releases after pre-deployment testing could not rule out meaningful biological weapons uplift. The dual-use tension is acute: AI is also accelerating drug discovery and pandemic preparedness. [Rose Hadshar's work](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/KENtuXySHJgxsH2Qk/managing-catastrophic-misuse-without-robust-ais) on managing catastrophic misuse without robust AIs proposes structural governance approaches.

open_problems.problem: At what capability level AI models will provide meaningful bioweapon uplift, and whether we'll detect this threshold in time
open_problems.best_answer: [RAND's red-team study](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA2977-2.html) (2024) found no statistically significant difference in bioweapon plan viability with current LLMs. But [Epoch AI](https://epoch.ai/gradient-updates/do-the-biorisk-evaluations-of-ai-labs-actually-measure-the-risk-of-developing-bioweapons) devastatingly argues that lab evaluations are testing knowledge rather than the operational bottlenecks that actually limit bioweapon development — meaning we may be measuring the wrong thing entirely.
open_problems.source: Epoch AI, 'Do Biorisk Evaluations Actually Measure the Risk?' 2025
open_problems.critique: The RAND study is reassuring about current models but tested only a snapshot in time. The Epoch AI critique is devastating to confidence in current evaluation methods. The gap between knowledge tests (where models excel) and operational capability (where they don't yet help) is the critical uncertainty. No study has convincingly established where the crossover point is.

open_problems.problem: How to distinguish between genuine biosecurity safeguards and safety-washing
open_problems.best_answer: [Ren et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21792) (NeurIPS 2024) demonstrated that most AI safety benchmarks — including those used for biosafety — are highly correlated with general capabilities rather than measuring a distinct safety dimension. Capability improvements can be misrepresented as safety progress.
open_problems.source: Ren et al., 'Safetywashing: Do AI Safety Benchmarks Actually Measure Safety Progress?' NeurIPS 2024
open_problems.critique: The most rigorous empirical treatment of safetywashing. The core finding is well-evidenced and alarming. But it identifies the problem more than it solves it. Biosecurity-specific verification is even harder because the ground truth (actual bioweapon creation) cannot be ethically tested.

open_problems.problem: Whether structured-access models (API-only, no open weights) can prevent misuse while enabling beneficial research
open_problems.best_answer: Anthropic's RSP v3.0 (Feb 2026) is the most developed structured-access implementation for biosecurity. But [RAND Europe](https://www.rand.org/randeurope/research/projects/2024/ai-risk-index.html) found that 82.5% of state-of-the-art AI bio tools had at least one open-source component — suggesting structured access to frontier LLMs may not matter when specialized biological design tools are freely available.
open_problems.source: RAND Europe, 'Global Risk Index for AI-enabled Biological Tools,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The 82.5% finding fundamentally undermines the premise of structured access as a standalone solution. Anthropic's RSP is detailed but unilateral and self-enforcing. The broader open-source biology tool ecosystem makes API-only restrictions on LLMs necessary but far from sufficient.

open_problems.problem: How to govern open-weight models where safeguards can be fine-tuned away
open_problems.best_answer: The most promising technical result is '[Deep Ignorance](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.06601v1)' (Oxford/EleutherAI/UK AISI, 2025): filtering pretraining data to make models genuinely ignorant of dangerous knowledge, proving 10x more resistant to adversarial fine-tuning than prior methods. [Casper et al.](https://openreview.net/pdf/6c3f22328222a570d6de6888ffe96ed156092559.pdf) survey 16 open technical problems across the lifecycle.
open_problems.source: 'Deep Ignorance: Filtering Pretraining Data Builds Tamper-Resistant Safeguards,' Oxford/EleutherAI/UK AISI, 2025
open_problems.critique: Deep Ignorance works for knowledge that can be cleanly separated from general capabilities (e.g., specific synthesis routes) but cannot address dual-use capabilities inherently entangled with beneficial uses (e.g., general chemistry reasoning). The 16 open problems in Casper et al. underscore how far the field is from solved.

open_problems.problem: International coordination on AI biosecurity standards
open_problems.best_answer: The BWC Working Group addressed AI for the first time substantively in 2025. [RAND Europe](https://www.rand.org/randeurope/research/projects/2025/biosecurity-governance.html) provides a scenario-based governance framework. A 2024 Trieste meeting brought 38 experts from 32 countries to deliberate on AI and biosecurity.
open_problems.source: RAND Europe, 'Biosecurity Governance Across Uncertain AI Futures,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The BWC is the most legitimate international forum but notoriously slow, under-resourced, and has no verification mechanism. Its prohibition norms predate AI and adapting them is politically fraught. International coordination on AI biosecurity remains embryonic.

agendas.name: Dual-Use & General Misuse
agendas.description: Broader risks of AI misuse including disinformation, cyberattacks, and general dual-use concerns.

papers.title: The Malicious Use of Artificial Intelligence: Forecasting, Prevention, and Mitigation
