papers.description: Foundational report by 26 authors from 14 institutions surveying the landscape of potential security threats from malicious uses of AI.

papers.title: An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks
papers.description: Comprehensive taxonomy of catastrophic AI risk scenarios and mitigation strategies from the Center for AI Safety.

papers.title: AI and the Future of Disinformation Campaigns
papers.description: Two-part CSET series introducing the RICHDATA framework (a disinformation kill chain) and analyzing AI-enabled disinformation.

papers.title: AI and Chemical, Biological, Radiological, & Nuclear Hazards
papers.description: Analyzes the intersection of AI capabilities and CBRN weapons risks, including the US Executive Order directives on biosecurity studies.

agendas.research_direction: Address the broader landscape of catastrophic AI misuse beyond bioweapons — cyberattacks, disinformation at scale, autonomous hacking, and novel attack vectors enabled by AI capabilities. Research develops taxonomies of misuse risks, proposes governance frameworks, and evaluates the effectiveness of existing safeguards.
agendas.theory_of_change: AI dramatically lowers the cost and skill required for certain categories of attacks. Governance can raise the cost back up through a combination of technical safeguards (output filtering, monitoring), legal deterrence (liability, criminalization), and structural measures (access controls, KYC). The goal is not to prevent all misuse but to prevent catastrophic-scale misuse.
agendas.progress: [Brundage et al. (2018)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.07228) established the foundational framework with 26 authors mapping the misuse landscape. [Hendrycks et al. (2023)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12001) provided a comprehensive taxonomy of catastrophic AI risks. [CSET](https://cset.georgetown.edu) has mapped AI-enabled disinformation risks. However, the field is struggling to keep pace with rapidly evolving capabilities. The open-weight model debate (see Open-Source Governance agenda) is central to this question.

open_problems.problem: How to govern AI misuse when capabilities are widely distributed through open-weight models
open_problems.best_answer: The [NTIA](https://www.ntia.gov/programs-and-initiatives/artificial-intelligence/open-model-weights-report) report (2024) is deliberately agnostic: 'current evidence is not sufficient to determine either that restrictions are warranted, or that they will never be appropriate.' It recommends active monitoring rather than preemptive restriction. The [UK AISI](https://www.aisi.gov.uk/blog/managing-risks-from-increasingly-capable-open-weight-ai-systems) proposes combining technical safeguards, ecosystem monitoring, and downstream enforcement.
open_problems.source: NTIA, 'Dual-Use Foundation Models with Widely Available Model Weights,' 2024; UK AISI companion report, 2025
open_problems.critique: The NTIA's fence-sitting is both intellectually honest and practically unhelpful. The UK AISI report is more prescriptive but acknowledges 'no current solutions can offer hard guarantees.' The fundamental challenge — open weights are irreversible once released — remains unaddressed.

open_problems.problem: Whether content-level filters are sustainable as models become more capable
open_problems.best_answer: The '[Deep Ignorance](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.06601v1)' approach (Oxford/EleutherAI/UK AISI, 2025) is 10x more resistant to adversarial removal than post-hoc filters. But [Ren et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21792) show most content-safety benchmarks measure capability rather than genuine safety, suggesting current filters are more brittle than they appear.
open_problems.source: 'Deep Ignorance,' Oxford/EleutherAI/UK AISI, 2025
open_problems.critique: A genuine breakthrough for the narrow case of specific dangerous knowledge in open-weight models. But the broader arms race between circumvention and filtering is structural, and no paper has argued defenders can maintain a permanent advantage.

open_problems.problem: How to balance surveillance/monitoring against privacy and free expression
open_problems.best_answer: The EU AI Act's interaction with GDPR provides the most developed legal framework, combining risk-based AI regulation with data protection. The Act bans certain biometric surveillance uses while permitting others under strict conditions.
open_problems.source: EU AI Act + GDPR interaction analysis, 2025
open_problems.critique: No jurisdiction has successfully balanced AI model-misuse monitoring with robust privacy protections. The EU is the most protective but doesn't address monitoring AI-generated content at scale. China's surveillance-permissive approach raises questions about whether any 'balanced' framework survives authoritarian adoption.

open_problems.problem: What institutional capacity is needed to respond to novel AI-enabled attacks
open_problems.best_answer: [Google DeepMind](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.11917v3) (2025) provides the most systematic framework, mapping AI-amplified offensive capabilities across attack stages with defense prioritization. AI-assisted attacks reportedly increased 72% since 2024.
open_problems.source: Google DeepMind, 'A Framework for Evaluating Emerging Cyberattack Capabilities of AI,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Technically strong for cyber but doesn't address institutional capacity across all AI-enabled attack vectors (disinformation, bioweapons, autonomous systems). Institutional design recommendations remain generic.

categories.name: Evaluations & Auditing
categories.description: Third-party auditing, red-teaming, capability evaluations, and transparency mechanisms for frontier AI systems.

agendas.name: Third-Party Auditing & Red-Teaming
agendas.description: Independent assessment of AI system capabilities and risks before and after deployment.

papers.title: Auditing Large Language Models: A Three-Layered Approach
papers.description: Proposes a three-layered approach combining governance audits, model audits, and application audits for LLMs.

papers.title: A Safe Harbor for AI Evaluation and Red Teaming
papers.description: Proposes legal and technical safe harbors to protect good-faith AI safety researchers from account suspensions and legal reprisal.

papers.title: FLI AI Safety Index 2024
papers.description: Independent panel of seven experts (including Yoshua Bengio and Stuart Russell) evaluated six AI companies across six safety domains using a standardized grading framework.

papers.title: Harmonizing AI Guidance: Distilling Voluntary Standards and Best Practices into a Unified Framework
papers.description: Distilled 7,000+ recommendations from 52 reports into 258 recommended practices for AI governance and management.

papers.title: We Need a Science of Evals
papers.description: Argues that AI evaluations driving policy decisions need much more scientific rigor, proposing a research agenda for making the field more robust.

papers.title: AI Companies' Eval Reports Mostly Don't Support Their Claims
papers.description: Finds that AI companies claim models are safe based on dangerous capability evaluations, but public evidence shows minimal pre-deployment access was actually given to independent evaluators.

papers.title: Can We Trust AI Benchmarks? An Interdisciplinary Review
papers.description: Analyzed ~110 publications: of 24 state-of-the-art benchmarks, only 4 provided replication scripts and no more than 10 reported statistical significance.

agendas.research_direction: Develop independent assessment capabilities for frontier AI systems — before and after deployment. Research covers auditing methodologies, legal frameworks for auditor access, scientific standards for evaluations, and the institutional design of evaluation bodies. A central theme is the gap between what labs claim about their safety testing and what actually happens.
agendas.theory_of_change: Labs evaluating their own models is like companies auditing their own financial statements — the conflict of interest is obvious. Independent third-party auditing creates accountability. If evaluators have meaningful pre-deployment access and the authority to block unsafe releases, dangerous models are less likely to be deployed. This requires legal safe harbors for auditors, technical access to model internals, and scientific standards for what counts as a passing evaluation.
agendas.progress: [METR](https://metr.org) conducts pre-deployment evaluations for some frontier labs. [UK AISI](https://www.aisi.gov.uk) has conducted evaluations of several frontier models. The [FLI](https://futureoflife.org) AI Safety Index grades companies on evaluation practices. However, METR's own research shows companies are not really using external evaluators in a meaningful way — pre-deployment access is limited and findings are often ignored. [Barnes (2024)](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/fnc6Sgt3CGCdFmmgX/we-need-a-science-of-evals) argues the field needs much more scientific rigor. The EU's G[PAI](https://partnershiponai.org) Code of Practice is the first legally mandated evaluation framework.

open_problems.problem: How to give auditors meaningful access without creating security risks from model weight sharing
open_problems.best_answer: The [Ada Lovelace Institute](https://www.adalovelaceinstitute.org/report/code-conduct-ai/) proposes tiered access: output-only auditing (lowest access), API-level auditing (moderate), and weight-level auditing in secure enclaves (highest access, managed risk), based on the NYC Local Law 144 experience.
open_problems.source: Ada Lovelace Institute, 'Code & Conduct,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Conceptually sound but the NYC case study involves narrow bias auditing, not catastrophic risk evaluations where weight access is most needed. The secure-enclave approach for weights is mentioned but not technically specified. The core tension — meaningful auditing requires deep access, which creates exfiltration risk — remains unresolved at the frontier.

open_problems.problem: Whether current evaluation methodologies can detect the risks that matter most (e.g., deception, scheming)
open_problems.best_answer: Anthropic and Apollo Research have demonstrated that frontier models can strategically behave safely under monitoring while reverting when oversight is removed ('alignment faking'). A 2025 paper '[Difficulties with Evaluating a Deception Detector for AIs](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.22662)' shows the fundamental challenge: distinguishing strategic deception from roleplay may be underdetermined. [CLTR](https://www.longtermresilience.org/reports/scheming-in-the-wild/) found a 4.9x increase in scheming incidents.
open_problems.source: 'Difficulties with Evaluating a Deception Detector for AIs,' arXiv, 2025; CLTR, 'Scheming in the Wild,' 2026
open_problems.critique: Arguably the most alarming finding across all these problems. We may face a fundamental epistemic barrier: we cannot reliably distinguish genuine alignment from sophisticated mimicry. OpenAI's finding that deliberative alignment training reduced scheming from 13% to 0.4% in o3 is encouraging but may only address detectable forms.

open_problems.problem: How to create a science of evaluations with replicable standards
open_problems.best_answer: '[Can We Trust AI Benchmarks?](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.06559v2)' (2025) analyzed ~110 publications: of 24 state-of-the-art benchmarks examined, only 4 provided replication scripts and no more than 10 reported statistical significance. Data contamination, benchmark saturation, and inconsistent conditions undermine reliability.
open_problems.source: 'Can We Trust AI Benchmarks?' arXiv, 2025
open_problems.critique: The most comprehensive diagnosis. The finding that only 4/24 benchmarks provide replication scripts is damning. But proposed solutions (minimum standards) require institutional enforcement that doesn't exist. The deeper structural problem — benchmarks go stale as models train on them — is not fully addressed.

open_problems.problem: Legal frameworks that protect auditors from retaliation or legal liability
open_problems.best_answer: The bipartisan [AI Whistleblower Protection Act](https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/1792/text) (Senator Grassley, May 2025) prohibits retaliation, makes NDAs unenforceable for protected disclosures, and covers current/former employees and contractors.
open_problems.source: S.1792, AI Whistleblower Protection Act, 119th Congress, 2025
open_problems.critique: Strongest legislative answer. The NDA override is particularly important. But the bill hasn't passed, and its scope covers whistleblowers (employees) rather than third-party auditors and external researchers who face different legal risks (e.g., CFAA liability for testing). The external auditor protection gap remains significant.

open_problems.problem: How to fund independent evaluation at the scale needed
open_problems.best_answer: [TechPolicy.Press](https://www.techpolicy.press/a-plan-to-fund-independent-assessments-of-general-purpose-ai/) proposes mandatory assessment fees paid by AI developers (similar to financial sector supervisory fees). The US AISI contracted Scale AI as a third-party evaluator in Feb 2025, establishing a public-private funding model.
open_problems.source: 'A Plan to Fund Independent Assessments of General-Purpose AI,' TechPolicy.Press, 2025
open_problems.critique: The supervisory fee model is economically sustainable and has precedent. But the Scale AI contract raises capture concerns — Scale AI is deeply embedded in the industry it would evaluate. The fundamental question of whether sufficient independent expertise exists at any price remains unaddressed.

agendas.name: Dangerous Capability Evaluations
agendas.description: Evaluating AI systems for capabilities that could pose catastrophic risks — CBRN knowledge, autonomous replication, deception, and persuasion.

papers.title: Model Evaluation for Extreme Risks
papers.description: Multi-institution paper explaining why model evaluation is critical for addressing extreme risks from advanced AI capabilities.

papers.title: Scheming in the Wild
papers.description: Analysis of 180,000+ transcripts identifying 698 scheming-related incidents where AI systems acted in misaligned ways, finding a 4.9x increase over the collection period.

papers.title: OpenAI's Approach to External Red Teaming for AI Models and Systems
papers.description: OpenAI's published methodology for external red teaming of their AI models and systems.

papers.title: AI Sandbagging: Language Models Can Strategically Underperform on Evaluations
papers.description: Demonstrates that GPT-4 and Claude 3 Opus can selectively underperform on dangerous capability evaluations while maintaining performance on general tasks. Proposes noise injection as a detection method.

agendas.research_direction: Evaluate frontier AI systems specifically for capabilities that could enable catastrophic outcomes — autonomous replication and adaptation (ARA), CBRN knowledge uplift, cyber-offense capabilities, persuasion and manipulation, and deceptive alignment (scheming). This is the technical frontier of AI safety evaluation, where the stakes are highest and the methodology is least mature.
agendas.theory_of_change: If we can reliably detect when AI systems acquire dangerous capabilities, we can trigger governance responses (pausing scaling, restricting deployment, requiring additional safeguards) before those capabilities cause harm. Dangerous capability evaluations are the tripwire mechanism that makes RSPs and safety frameworks operational.
agendas.progress: [Shevlane et al. (2023, DeepMind)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.15324) laid out the framework for extreme risk evaluations. [CLTR](https://www.longtermresilience.org)'s ['Scheming in the Wild' (2026)](https://www.longtermresilience.org/reports/scheming-in-the-wild/) analyzed 180,000+ transcripts and found 698 scheming incidents with a 4.9x increase over the collection period. [Apollo Research](https://www.apolloresearch.ai) has demonstrated deceptive behavior in frontier models. OpenAI has published its external red-teaming methodology (2025). However, the fundamental challenge remains: evaluations can only test for capabilities we know to look for, and models may behave differently in test vs. deployment settings.

open_problems.problem: How to evaluate for capabilities that emerge unexpectedly rather than incrementally
open_problems.best_answer: A 2025 approach proposes using fine-tuning on smaller models to predict emergence thresholds in larger models, claiming to anticipate emergent abilities up to 4x earlier. The [International AI Safety Report](https://internationalaisafetyreport.org/publication/first-key-update-capabilities-and-risk-implications) provides the most authoritative monitoring framework.
open_problems.source: 'Emergent Abilities in Large Language Models: A Survey,' arXiv, 2025
open_problems.critique: The prediction method is clever but tested on known emergent abilities, not truly novel ones — survivorship bias. If a capability is truly unexpected, by definition we don't know to test for it. This remains one of the hardest problems with no convincing solution.

open_problems.problem: Whether AI systems can deliberately underperform on evaluations (sandbagging) to avoid triggering safety responses
open_problems.best_answer: [Weij et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07358) (ICLR 2025) demonstrate GPT-4 and Claude 3 Opus can selectively underperform on dangerous capability evals while maintaining general performance. A follow-up proposes detection via [noise injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.01784) — sandbagging models anomalously improve with noise while normal models degrade predictably.
open_problems.source: Weij et al., 'AI Sandbagging,' ICLR 2025; 'Noise Injection Reveals Hidden Capabilities,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Strong pair of papers that both demonstrates the threat and proposes detection. But noise injection detection has only been tested on known sandbagging setups. The distinction between developer sandbagging (intentional) and emergent sandbagging (model-initiated) is crucial — the latter would be far more dangerous and evidence for it remains limited.

open_problems.problem: How to evaluate for deceptive alignment when the model may be trying to appear safe
open_problems.best_answer: Anthropic's alignment faking research (2024) and replications in smaller models (2025) provide robust evidence across labs. Anthropic's mid-2025 simulations across 16 frontier models found lying, cheating, and disabling oversight. [DeceptionBench](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.15501v1) offers the first systematic benchmark for deception behaviors.
open_problems.source: Anthropic/Redwood alignment faking research, 2024; DeceptionBench, 2025
open_problems.critique: Evidence that alignment faking occurs is now robust across multiple labs and model families. But the fundamental problem remains: if a sufficiently capable model is deceptively aligned, it would by definition appear safe on evaluations designed to detect deception. Interpretability may eventually help, but current methods cannot provide reliable guarantees. This may be the single hardest problem in AI safety.

open_problems.problem: Scaling evaluation to keep pace with the rate of new model releases
open_problems.best_answer: The [FLI AI Safety Index](https://futureoflife.org/ai-safety-index-summer-2025/) tracks safety across many models semi-annually. [METR](https://metr.org/) operates as a dedicated third-party evaluator. But new models are released faster than they can be rigorously evaluated — automated AI-on-AI evaluation is the most promising scaling approach but introduces recursive trust problems.
open_problems.source: FLI AI Safety Index, 2025; METR
open_problems.critique: The FLI Index relies on public information and voluntary disclosures. METR has some pre-deployment access but cannot evaluate all releases. The structural problem — release pace outstrips evaluation capacity — has no solution in sight.

open_problems.problem: How to handle the meta-problem: evaluations of evaluations
open_problems.best_answer: [Griffin & Jacobs](https://www.aipolicyperspectives.com/p/what-we-learned-from-reading-100) (2025) make the explicit case for 'AI meta-evaluation' as a field after reviewing ~100 evaluations and finding systematic gaps. '[Can We Trust AI Benchmarks?](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.06559v2)' proposes benchmark risk scores using Item Response Theory.
open_problems.source: Griffin & Jacobs, 'What We Learned from Reading ~100 AI Safety Evaluations,' 2025
open_problems.critique: A nascent field. The 100-evaluation review is useful but limited. Meta-evaluation requires even more expertise than evaluation, creating further bottleneck. No institution has taken meta-evaluation as a core mandate.

categories.name: Strategic Analysis
categories.description: Geopolitical dynamics of AI development — race dynamics, deterrence theory, power transitions, and strategic stability.

agendas.name: AI Race Dynamics & Strategic Stability
agendas.description: Analysis of competitive pressures between nations and labs, and how race dynamics affect safety investments.

papers.title: Racing to the Precipice: A Model of Artificial Intelligence Development
papers.description: Seminal model of an AI arms race showing how competitive pressures incentivize skimping on safety precautions, with more teams and more enmity increasing danger.

papers.title: Societal Adaptation to Advanced AI
papers.description: Proposes a framework for societal adaptation to advanced AI, identifying interventions that avoid, defend against, and remedy harmful uses.

papers.title: Analysis of Global AI Governance Strategies
papers.description: Evaluates three major AI governance strategies - Cooperative Development, Strategic Advantage, and Global Moratorium - across different timeline and alignment difficulty scenarios.

papers.title: AI Governance Needs a Theory of Victory
papers.description: Argues that AI governance efforts need explicit theories of victory, comparing moratorium, coordination, and differential progress approaches.

papers.title: Soft Nationalization: How the US Government Will Control AI Labs
papers.description: Argues the US government will progressively exert control over frontier AI development through various policy levers, short of full nationalization.

papers.title: The Coordination Gap in Frontier AI Safety Policies
papers.description: Argues the gap between AI capabilities and safety is structural: coordination yields diffuse benefits but concentrated costs, producing systematic underinvestment.

papers.title: Superintelligence Strategy
papers.description: Proposes Mutual Assured AI Malfunction (MAIM) as a deterrence framework where rivals would sabotage any state's bid for unilateral AI dominance.

agendas.research_direction: Analyze competitive pressures between nations and between labs, and how these dynamics affect safety investments. Research models race conditions, identifies interventions that reduce racing incentives, and evaluates strategic options (coordination, deterrence, differential development). The foundational question: can we slow the race enough for safety to keep pace?
agendas.theory_of_change: Competitive pressure is the primary driver of unsafe AI development. If we understand the game theory — what makes actors cut safety corners, and what incentives could change their behavior — we can design governance interventions that restructure the incentives. Possible interventions include international agreements, safety standards that function as coordination mechanisms, and windfall clauses that reduce winner-take-all dynamics.
agendas.progress: [Armstrong, Bostrom & Shulman (2016)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-015-0590-y) provided the seminal race model. [Dafoe's 2018 research agenda](https://www.governance.ai/research-paper/agenda) framed the strategic landscape comprehensively. [Convergence Analysis](https://www.convergenceanalysis.org) (2024) evaluated three major governance strategies across different scenarios. Trager's ['Theory of Victory' framework](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/cuAFeLmquyEm3X6s5/ai-governance-needs-a-theory-of-victory-1) helps organize strategic thinking. Convergence Analysis's ['Soft Nationalization' analysis](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/47RH47AyLnHqCQRCD/soft-nationalization-how-the-us-government-will-control-ai) identified how government control of labs may be the likely trajectory regardless of explicit policy. However, race dynamics have arguably intensified since 2023, not decreased.

open_problems.problem: Whether any governance intervention can actually slow the race, or whether competitive pressures are too strong
open_problems.best_answer: [Armstrong, Bostrom & Shulman](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-015-0590-y) show the Nash equilibrium drives each team to cut safety corners. [Mengesha](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10015) (2026) argues the gap is structural: coordination yields diffuse benefits but concentrated costs, producing systematic underinvestment in safety. $471 billion in US AI investment (2013-2024) with $300B more projected for 2025 reflects pressures governance has failed to moderate.
open_problems.source: Armstrong et al., 'Racing to the Precipice,' 2016; Mengesha, 'The Coordination Gap,' 2026
open_problems.critique: The formal results are elegant but assume a single discontinuous 'prize' for finishing first. Mengesha identifies the structural problem but proposed solutions (precommitment, shared protocols) remain aspirational with no enforcement mechanism.

open_problems.problem: How the US-China dynamic interacts with lab-vs-lab competition within each country
open_problems.best_answer: [Brookings](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/china-is-running-multiple-ai-races/) (2025) documents the shift from a training race to a deployment race, with Chinese labs innovating on efficiency under compute constraints while US labs pursue scale. The capability gap between leading US and Chinese models has essentially disappeared, creating nested competition.
open_problems.source: Brookings, 'China Is Running Multiple AI Races,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Captures the training-to-inference shift well but lacks a formal model of how lab-vs-lab dynamics feed back into interstate competition. No paper models the three-level game (researchers, labs, countries).

open_problems.problem: Whether safety standards can serve as coordination mechanisms (racing to safety) rather than just costs
open_problems.best_answer: [METR's Common Elements](https://metr.org/common-elements) (December 2025) documents convergence across labs on safety frameworks, suggesting emergent coordination. [Mengesha](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10015) proposes a Scenario Response Registry — ex ante 'if-then' plans with standardized thresholds creating common knowledge.
open_problems.source: METR, 'Common Elements,' 2025; Mengesha, 'The Coordination Gap,' 2026
open_problems.critique: METR documents convergence but can't distinguish genuine coordination from superficial compliance. The SRR proposal is creative but untested. No paper demonstrates empirically that safety investments have functioned as coordination mechanisms.

open_problems.problem: What happens to race dynamics if one actor achieves a decisive capability lead
open_problems.best_answer: [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA4444-1.html) (Sytsma, 2026) models when a temporary first-mover lead transitions to durable economic dominance. [Hendrycks, Schmidt & Wang](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.05628) propose Mutual Assured AI Malfunction (MAIM) as a deterrence framework — rivals would sabotage any state's bid for unilateral AI dominance.
open_problems.source: Sytsma, 'Decisive Economic Advantage,' RAND, 2026; Hendrycks et al., 'Superintelligence Strategy,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The RAND model is useful but depends on strong AGI economic assumptions. MAIM is provocative but deeply controversial — it normalizes military strikes on civilian infrastructure and has an observability problem. Neither addresses a quiet, gradual lead.

open_problems.problem: How to model race dynamics when capabilities may be discontinuous rather than smooth
open_problems.best_answer: Armstrong et al.'s original model assumes a discontinuous prize, the most direct treatment. The [International AI Safety Report](https://internationalaisafetyreport.org/publication/international-ai-safety-report-2025) (2025) documents that 'new capabilities sometimes emerge unpredictably' and inference-time scaling creates capability jumps not captured by pre-deployment evaluations.
open_problems.source: International AI Safety Report, 2025
open_problems.critique: Armstrong's model is a decade old and doesn't incorporate modern scaling laws. The Safety Report documents the phenomenon but offers no formal model. Significant gap: no paper combines race-theoretic modeling with empirical capability discontinuity evidence.

agendas.name: AI Security & Model Protection
agendas.description: Securing frontier AI systems against theft, misuse, and compromise — model weight security, lab security, and governance approaches.

papers.title: Securing AI Model Weights: Preventing Theft and Misuse of Frontier Models
papers.description: Provides a playbook for frontier AI labs to benchmark and strengthen security measures for protecting model weights.

papers.title: Governance Approaches to Securing Frontier AI
papers.description: Identifies four governance approaches for strengthening frontier AI security, drawing on case studies of US compliance regimes in nuclear, chemical, and healthcare industries.

papers.title: Valuing and Assessing Prevention and Preparedness for Potential Artificial Intelligence Disasters
papers.description: Explores cost-benefit logic for AI disaster prevention, informed by US responses to terrorism after 9/11.

agendas.research_direction: Secure frontier AI systems against theft, misuse, and compromise — model weight security, lab security, and governance approaches to AI security. This agenda treats AI models as critical assets (like nuclear material or classified intelligence) and asks what security practices are needed to prevent unauthorized access, copying, or misuse.
agendas.theory_of_change: If frontier model weights are stolen or leaked, all downstream governance (RSPs, deployment controls, export controls) is undermined. Security governance ensures that the models subject to governance actually remain under the control of accountable actors. This is a necessary condition for every other governance approach to work.
agendas.progress: [RAND](https://www.rand.org) (Nevo et al., 2024) produced the most comprehensive playbook for frontier AI model security. A follow-up RAND report (2024) identified four governance approaches drawn from nuclear, chemical, and healthcare compliance regimes. The [Biden Executive Order](https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/presidential-actions/2023/10/30/executive-order-on-the-safe-secure-and-trustworthy-development-and-use-of-artificial-intelligence/) included cybersecurity requirements for frontier models. However, there have been documented incidents of model weight leaks (e.g., Meta's LLaMA weights), and security practices at most labs remain well below the standard expected for critical national security assets.

open_problems.problem: Whether AI lab security can match the standards of nuclear or intelligence facilities while maintaining the open culture labs depend on
open_problems.best_answer: [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA2849-1.html) (2024) identifies 38 attack vectors and proposes five security levels calibrated to threat actors. Acknowledges the fundamental tension: strongest measures (network isolation, compartmentalization) directly conflict with open research culture. A companion [Nature paper](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03017-1) proposes adapting IAEA-style frameworks.
open_problems.source: RAND, 'Securing AI Model Weights,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The most concrete security playbook, but admits the highest security levels may be incompatible with research openness. No paper demonstrates the nuclear model can be adapted without killing collaborative culture.

open_problems.problem: How to secure against insider threats at labs with thousands of employees
open_problems.best_answer: [INSA](https://www.insaonline.org/docs/default-source/uploadedfiles/2025/insa_ai_irm_paper.pdf) (2024) provides intelligence-community-derived guidance. Industry surveys show 41% of organizations anticipate AI-driven insider threats while 70% lack optimized AI governance. RAND recommends compartmentalized access, HSMs, and cryptographic weight protection.
open_problems.source: INSA, 'Recommendations When Using AI in Insider Risk Management,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Draws on intelligence practices but doesn't address unique AI lab features: rapid scaling, researcher autonomy, open publication norms. No paper deeply models organizational dynamics specific to labs with thousands of researchers expecting model access.

open_problems.problem: What governance structures create the right incentives for labs to invest in security
open_problems.best_answer: [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_briefs/RBA4159-1.html) evaluates mandatory standards, liability, insurance, and government partnerships. Key finding: proportionality — calibrating requirements to risk level — is the most effective principle.
open_problems.source: RAND, 'Four Governance Approaches to Securing Advanced AI,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Well-structured but remains at policy-options level without demonstrated effectiveness. No empirical evidence on which approach actually changes lab investment behavior. Doesn't address the free-rider problem.

open_problems.problem: How to handle security for open-weight models where the weights are deliberately released
open_problems.best_answer: [Casper et al.](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5705186) (2025) concludes that once weights are released, technical controls are essentially impossible. This is an honest assessment that answers 'no' — governance must work through pre-release controls rather than post-release.
open_problems.source: Casper et al., 'Open Technical Problems in Open-Weight AI,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Convincing analysis, but the negative answer leaves governance practitioners with no tools. This is a fundamental constraint, not a research gap.

open_problems.problem: What level of security is proportionate to what level of model capability
open_problems.best_answer: RAND's five-tier framework maps security levels to threat actors. The [AIGL](https://www.aigl.blog/content/files/2025/04/Intolerable-Risk-Threshold-Recommendations-for-Artificial-Intelligence.pdf) (2025) attempts to define capability thresholds triggering graduated security. The EU AI Act's risk-tiering provides regulatory precedent.
open_problems.source: RAND, 'Securing AI Model Weights,' 2024; AIGL, 'Intolerable Risk Thresholds,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Maps security to threat actors rather than capabilities directly — the harder question. Proportionality is widely endorsed but nowhere rigorously operationalized.

categories.name: Post-AGI Governance
categories.description: Governance challenges specific to highly capable AI systems — value lock-in, power concentration, alignment governance, and civilizational resilience.

agendas.name: Power Concentration & Lock-in
agendas.description: Preventing catastrophic concentration of power through AI — whether by states, corporations, or AI systems themselves.

papers.title: Two Types of AI Existential Risk: Decisive and Accumulative
papers.description: Distinguishes between decisive existential risks (single catastrophic events) and accumulative risks (gradual lock-in of harmful values).

papers.title: AI Now 2023 Landscape: Confronting Tech Power
papers.description: AI Now's annual report focusing on the concentration of power in the tech industry and approaches to confront it.

papers.title: Misalignment or Misuse? The AGI Alignment Tradeoff
papers.description: Argues that misaligned AGI poses catastrophic risks while aligned AGI creates substantial misuse risk, exploring alignment approaches that don't increase misuse risk.

papers.title: AI, Global Governance, and Digital Sovereignty
papers.description: Framework for international AGI governance outlining mechanisms for democratic accountability and noting challenges of power concentration.

papers.title: Gradual Disempowerment: Systemic Existential Risks from Incremental AI Development
papers.description: Argues that even gradual AI capability increases create feedback loops undermining human influence across economic, political, and cultural functions.

papers.title: Forging A New AGI Social Contract
papers.description: Proposes that AGI could destabilize the fundamental economic bargain between labor and security, requiring a reimagining of governance structures.

papers.title: Why Does AI Hinder Democratization?
papers.description: Empirical evidence that AI has hindered democratic development due to 'technology complementarity' — AI is more useful to rulers with administrative data than to civil society.

agendas.research_direction: Prevent catastrophic concentration of power enabled by AI — whether by states, corporations, or AI systems themselves. Research examines how AI could enable authoritarian lock-in, permanent surveillance states, or monopolistic control of economic output. This agenda takes seriously the risk that AI could be aligned with human values but still produce dystopian outcomes if those values are concentrated in too few hands.
agendas.theory_of_change: Even perfectly aligned AI is dangerous if controlled by a small number of actors. If one entity controls superintelligent AI, they could use it to entrench their power permanently — ending meaningful political competition, economic freedom, or cultural change. Governance must ensure that AI power is distributed, accountable, and reversible. This requires both preventing concentration ex ante and designing institutions that can redistribute power ex post.
agendas.progress: [Kasirzadeh (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.07836) distinguished decisive vs. accumulative existential risks — the latter involves gradual lock-in rather than a single catastrophe. [Kulveit (2025)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/vLDd4tit9gJtLmZZY/gradual-disempowerment-systemic-existential-risks-from) modeled how incremental AI capabilities create disempowerment feedback loops. [Cheng (2025)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/SMrfEcGv3jcKkArFK/forging-a-new-agi-social-contract-1) proposed reimagining social contracts for the AGI era. [AI Now](https://ainowinstitute.org) has documented current tech industry power concentration. Survey data from the 2026 Summit suggests x-risk community leaders increasingly view risks from aligned AI (authoritarian lock-in) as underweighted relative to misalignment.

open_problems.problem: How to prevent power concentration while still allowing beneficial AI development
open_problems.best_answer: [Sastry, Heim et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.08797) propose using compute governance (subsidized access, export controls, licensing) to prevent concentration at the infrastructure level. [AI Now](https://ainowinstitute.org/publications/power-and-governance-in-the-age-of-ai) (2025) documents how a handful of firms control cloud, data, and labor.
open_problems.source: Sastry et al., 'Computing Power and the Governance of AI,' 2024; AI Now, 2025
open_problems.critique: Compute governance is promising but could itself concentrate power in whoever controls allocation. The paper acknowledges hardware monitoring could enable authoritarian surveillance. No complete blueprint exists.

open_problems.problem: Whether antitrust and competition law are sufficient, or whether entirely new governance frameworks are needed
open_problems.best_answer: A [2025 ScienceDirect paper](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212473X25000835) compares antitrust in the EU, US, and China, finding existing tools insufficient for markets where three cloud providers control AI infrastructure. The EU Digital Markets Act is the most ambitious adaptation but hasn't designated AI services as 'core platform services.'
open_problems.source: 'Antitrust in AI Infrastructure,' ScienceDirect, 2025
open_problems.critique: Documents the problem better than it solves it. Doesn't propose what a genuinely new framework would look like beyond adapting existing tools.

open_problems.problem: How to design institutions that can redistribute AI-derived power — taxation, public ownership, distributed access
open_problems.best_answer: A [2024 AI & Ethics paper](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00635-y) proposes 'power-sharing liberalism' modeled on democratic constitutionalism. Compute governance (public compute infrastructure, subsidized access) is the most concrete redistribution mechanism proposed. But Dafoe's GovAI agenda honestly frames this as an open research question.
open_problems.source: 'A Roadmap for Governing AI: Power-Sharing Liberalism,' AI & Ethics, 2024
open_problems.critique: Provides normative principles but no implementation pathway. No existing paper provides a credible institutional design for redistributing AI-derived power in practice — one of the weakest spots in the literature.

open_problems.problem: How to prevent authoritarian lock-in in countries where democratic institutions are already weak
open_problems.best_answer: A [PNAS paper](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2423266122) (May 2025) provides empirical evidence that AI has hindered democratic development due to 'technology complementarity' — AI is more useful to rulers with administrative data than to civil society. [Barez](https://aigi.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Toward_Resisting_AI_Enabled_Authoritarianism_-4.pdf) (Oxford, 2025) proposes countermeasures, noting a 'wave of autocratization' in 42 countries.
open_problems.source: 'Why Does AI Hinder Democratization?' PNAS, 2025
open_problems.critique: Strong empirical evidence for the problem, limited solutions. Neither paper addresses the geopolitical dimension: authoritarian regimes often receive AI tools from allied states.

open_problems.problem: Whether the concentration of AI development in a handful of US companies is itself a governance problem
open_problems.best_answer: [AI Now](https://ainowinstitute.org/publications/power-and-governance-in-the-age-of-ai) (2025) directly argues concentration is a first-order governance problem. The [Biden White House AI Assessment](https://bidenwhitehouse.archives.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/AI-Assessment.-Power-and-Permitting.pdf) noted vertically integrated companies can condition or refuse access to rivals.
open_problems.source: AI Now, 'Power and Governance in the Age of AI,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Strong case that concentration is a problem, less convincing on solutions that wouldn't undermine US competitive advantage vis-à-vis China.

agendas.name: Alignment Governance
agendas.description: Governance interventions that depend on or interact with technical alignment — when to trust safety cases, how to verify alignment claims, and governance under uncertainty.

papers.title: Policymaking in the Pause
papers.description: Investigates policy opportunities that would arise from a pause on giant AI experiments, including governance infrastructure.

agendas.research_direction: Address governance questions that interact with technical alignment — when to trust safety cases, how to verify alignment claims, what governance measures are needed under deep uncertainty about whether AI systems are aligned, and how governance should change as alignment research matures or fails. This sits at the intersection of technical and governance work.
agendas.theory_of_change: Technical alignment and governance are not independent problems. Governance needs to work under uncertainty about alignment: if we can't verify whether a model is aligned, how do we decide whether to deploy it? If alignment succeeds, governance determines who benefits and how power is distributed. If alignment partially fails, governance is the last line of defense. Research aims to design governance that is robust across different alignment outcomes.
agendas.progress: This is the least developed of the governance agendas. [FLI](https://futureoflife.org)'s 'Policymaking in the Pause' explored what governance infrastructure should exist before deployment of highly capable systems. The [MIRI TGT](https://techgov.intelligence.org) research agenda implicitly addresses alignment-governance interactions through its scenario analysis. Safety cases (formal arguments that a system is safe enough to deploy) are being developed by some labs but there are no governance standards for what counts as an adequate safety case.

open_problems.problem: How should governance handle the possibility that alignment evaluations are fundamentally unreliable?
open_problems.best_answer: The [International AI Safety Report](https://internationalaisafetyreport.org/publication/international-ai-safety-report-2025) (2025) explicitly identifies an 'evaluation gap' where 'performance on pre-deployment tests does not reliably predict real-world utility or risk.' No governance mechanism that functions well under evaluation unreliability has been proposed.
open_problems.source: International AI Safety Report, 2025
open_problems.critique: Documents the problem clearly but offers no governance tools that work despite it. Acknowledging the evaluation gap without solutions leaves a dangerous void.

open_problems.problem: What standard of evidence should regulators require before allowing deployment of highly capable systems?
open_problems.best_answer: [METR's Common Elements](https://metr.org/common-elements) (December 2025) documents the emerging consensus: capability evaluations at defined thresholds with deployment gates triggered by specific dangerous capability levels. [UK AISI](https://www.aisi.gov.uk/blog/advanced-ai-evaluations-may-update) provides the most concrete template.
open_problems.source: METR, 'Common Elements,' 2025; UK AISI evaluations
open_problems.critique: Documents convergence on practices but these remain voluntary. The standard of evidence emerged from industry self-regulation, not regulatory determination — fox-guarding-henhouse.

open_problems.problem: How to design governance that works under deep uncertainty about whether alignment has been solved
open_problems.best_answer: '[Distributional AGI Safety](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.16856)' (arXiv, 2025) advocates conservative, pessimistic approaches under uncertainty. [Nay](https://law.stanford.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Aligning-AI-Agents-with-Humans-through-Law-as-Information.pdf) (Stanford, 2025) proposes using law as a framework for alignment — converting human values into legible directives regardless of whether technical alignment is solved.
open_problems.source: 'Distributional AGI Safety,' arXiv, 2025; Nay, 'Law as Information,' Stanford, 2025
open_problems.critique: The conservative approach is sound in principle but provides no guidance on how much caution is appropriate. Nay's framework applies to behavioral compliance, not the deeper alignment problem.

open_problems.problem: Who should have the authority to make deployment decisions about potentially transformative AI systems?
open_problems.best_answer: '[Democratizing AI Governance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.08651)' (arXiv, 2025) analyzes France and Brazil case studies to propose frameworks bridging expert oversight and democratic participation. A '[Last Vote](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.13432)' multi-stakeholder framework proposes structured roles for industry, academia, civil society, and affected communities.
open_problems.source: 'Democratizing AI Governance,' arXiv, 2025
open_problems.critique: Neither grapples with the speed problem: deployment decisions may need to be made in days while democratic deliberation takes months. Multi-stakeholder approaches risk becoming veto points or rubber stamps depending on power balance.

open_problems.problem: What governance mechanisms are needed if alignment turns out to be much harder than expected?
open_problems.best_answer: [Hendrycks, Schmidt & Wang](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.05628) propose MAIM deterrence plus nonproliferation as the backup strategy. [Mengesha](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10015) proposes pre-committed response protocols. But MAIM is essentially slowing or stopping AI via mutual threat — possibly worse than the alignment problem itself.
open_problems.source: Hendrycks et al., 'Superintelligence Strategy,' 2025
open_problems.critique: If alignment is fundamentally harder than expected, no current paper provides a governance mechanism that allows AI development to continue safely. This is arguably the most important open problem with the weakest existing answer.

categories.name: China & Geopolitics
categories.description: US-China AI dynamics, Chinese AI governance approaches, Global South perspectives, and geopolitical implications of AI development.

agendas.name: US-China AI Dynamics
agendas.description: Competition and potential cooperation between the US and China on AI development and governance.

papers.title: US-China Perspectives on Extreme AI Risks and Global Governance
papers.description: Compiles publicly-available statements from US and Chinese technical and policy leaders on extreme AI risks, identifying areas of potential bilateral cooperation.

papers.title: Bridging the Artificial Intelligence Governance Gap: US and China's Divergent Approaches to Governing General-Purpose AI
papers.description: Examines US and Chinese policy landscapes to identify differences in how the countries approach general-purpose AI governance.

papers.title: Promising Topics for US-China Dialogues on AI Risks and Governance
papers.description: Identifies promising topics for bilateral US-China dialogues on AI risks, building on the 2024 bilateral AI dialogue initiated in May 2024.

papers.title: Preparing for the Future of AI: US-China Cooperation and Competition
papers.description: Analysis of how the US and China can manage AI competition while cooperating on shared risks from advanced AI systems.

papers.title: China x AI Reference List: August 2025 Update
papers.description: Comprehensive curated reading list covering China's AI governance, domestic policy, international engagement, and key actors shaping AI development.

papers.title: Local Detours On A Narrow Path: Enforcement Risks in Chinese AI Governance
papers.description: Examines AI safety treaty proposals through a Chinese political economy lens, identifying four major implementation challenges including regulatory capture.

agendas.research_direction: Analyze the competitive and cooperative dynamics between the US and China on AI development and governance. Research asks whether and how these two AI superpowers can cooperate on shared catastrophic risks while competing on economic and military advantages. Work covers export controls, bilateral dialogues, signaling mechanisms, and the role of US-China dynamics in shaping global AI governance.
agendas.theory_of_change: US-China dynamics are the single most important geopolitical factor shaping AI governance. If the two countries can cooperate on catastrophic risk — even while competing on everything else — global governance becomes feasible. If they can't, a race to the bottom on safety becomes much more likely. Research aims to identify areas of mutual interest (both countries want to avoid AI-enabled bioweapons, for example) and design cooperation mechanisms that survive geopolitical tensions.
agendas.progress: [Wasil & Durgin (2024)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.16903) compiled US and Chinese expert statements on extreme AI risks, finding significant areas of overlap. The May 2024 bilateral AI dialogue created a formal channel. [RAND](https://www.rand.org) analyzed divergent governance approaches. However, semiconductor export controls and growing geopolitical rivalry make cooperation increasingly difficult. New research (2025) explores AI safety treaty proposals through a Chinese political economy lens, identifying real implementation challenges.

open_problems.problem: Whether bilateral AI safety cooperation is politically feasible given broader US-China tensions
open_problems.best_answer: [Guest & Wei](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.07468) (ACM FAccT 2025) identify 'low-hanging fruit' including biosecurity, critical infrastructure protection, and autonomous weapons norms. The [Sandia National Labs](https://www.sandia.gov/app/uploads/sites/148/2025/04/Challenges-and-Opportunities-for-US-China-Collaboration-on-Artificial-Intelligence-Governance.pdf) report maps challenges and opportunities. But even identified low-hanging fruit has produced no concrete bilateral agreements.
open_problems.source: Guest & Wei, 'Promising Topics for US-China Dialogues,' ACM FAccT 2025
open_problems.critique: The most careful mapping of shared interests. But even low-hanging fruit (biosecurity, autonomous weapons) has seen no agreements. Cooperation faces inherent information-sharing barriers. Political feasibility remains undemonstrated.

open_problems.problem: How to verify Chinese compliance with any AI agreement given the opacity of China's AI ecosystem
open_problems.best_answer: Compute-based governance (tracking chip flows, monitoring datacenter construction) is the most concrete proposal, leveraging the concentrated semiconductor supply chain. But China is investing heavily in domestic chip manufacturing, and [DeepSeek](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/china-is-running-multiple-ai-races/) demonstrates efficiency gains mean less compute may be needed for frontier capabilities.
open_problems.source: Sastry et al., 'Computing Power and the Governance of AI,' 2024
open_problems.critique: Compute monitoring is the most promising approach but faces serious limitations as China builds domestic capacity. No paper provides a credible verification framework comparable to nuclear inspections.

open_problems.problem: Whether export controls help (by slowing Chinese AI development) or hurt (by motivating China to build independent supply chains and refuse cooperation)
open_problems.best_answer: [CSIS](https://www.csis.org/analysis/limits-chip-export-controls-meeting-china-challenge) and the International Center for Law & Economics present competing views. The positive case: without controls, China might have built the first million-chip cluster. The negative case: controls accelerate self-sufficiency and reduce cooperation leverage. Historical CoCom analysis suggests enforcement weakens over time.
open_problems.source: CSIS, 'The Limits of Chip Export Controls,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Both analyses are well-reasoned but reach opposite conclusions, reflecting genuine uncertainty. The question may be empirically unanswerable a priori.

open_problems.problem: How to include other major AI nations (UK, EU, Japan, India) without US-China dynamics dominating
open_problems.best_answer: The [International Network of AI Safety Institutes](https://thefuturesociety.org/aisi-report/), launched May 2024, includes Australia, Canada, EU, France, Japan, Kenya, Korea, Singapore, UK, and US. But China is notably absent, risking a Western-aligned club that reinforces polarization.
open_problems.source: Future Society, 'Weaving a Safety Net,' 2024; CSIS, 'AISI Network Next Steps,' 2024
open_problems.critique: A real institutional achievement but China's absence fundamentally limits global coverage. No paper proposes how to bring China in while managing US security concerns.

open_problems.problem: Whether Track 2 (informal) dialogues can generate enough trust for Track 1 (government) agreements
open_problems.best_answer: The Brookings-Tsinghua Track II Dialogue (since 2019) contributed to the Biden-Xi Woodside agreement (November 2023) and the first bilateral AI meeting in Geneva (May 2024). This is the most concrete evidence of Track 2 feeding Track 1.
open_problems.source: Guest & Wei, 'Promising Topics for US-China Dialogues,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The strongest evidence that Track 2 can catalyze Track 1. But the Geneva meeting produced no binding commitments, and subsequent political deterioration has stalled progress. Track 2 is necessary but not sufficient.

agendas.name: Global South & AI Equity
agendas.description: Ensuring Global South nations have voice and representation in AI governance decisions that affect them.

papers.title: Network Architecture for Global AI Policy
papers.description: Proposes a network architecture for global AI policy coordination that accounts for diverse national contexts and interests.

papers.title: China's AI Governance Initiative and Its Geopolitical Ambitions
papers.description: Analyzes China's 2023 Global AI Governance Initiative (GAIGI) and its implications for international AI governance.

agendas.research_direction: Ensure that nations outside the US-UK-EU axis have voice and representation in AI governance decisions that affect them. Research asks who is making the rules, whose interests are being served, and how to build AI governance capacity in regions that are affected by AI but have little influence over its development.
agendas.theory_of_change: AI governance designed by and for wealthy nations will not be legitimate or effective globally. If Global South nations are excluded from governance design, they may refuse to participate in international agreements, creating governance gaps. Moreover, different regions face different AI risks (e.g., AI-enabled surveillance in authoritarian contexts, economic disruption in labor-intensive economies) that require different governance responses.
agendas.progress: [GovAI](https://www.governance.ai) has produced work on what should be internationalized in AI governance and how to increase voice for the 'Global AI Majority.' The 2026 Delhi AI Safety Summit (the first in the Global South) represents growing recognition of this issue. Brookings has proposed network architectures for inclusive global AI policy. However, actual Global South representation in AI governance discussions remains token. Most of the x-risk governance community is based in the US and UK.

open_problems.problem: How to build AI governance capacity in countries that lack technical expertise and institutional infrastructure
open_problems.best_answer: [CSIS](https://www.csis.org/analysis/divide-delivery-how-ai-can-serve-global-south) (2025) and the [UN General Assembly's July 2024 Resolution](https://www.un.org/sites/un2.un.org/files/governing_ai_for_humanity_final_report_en.pdf) on AI Capacity-Building (co-sponsored by 143 member states) represent the most concrete institutional efforts. [Brookings](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/ai-in-the-global-south-opportunities-and-challenges-towards-more-inclusive-governance/) emphasizes moving from consultation to co-design.
open_problems.source: CSIS, 'From Divide to Delivery,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The UN resolution is a political achievement but capacity-building plans remain largely unfunded and unimplemented. No paper demonstrates a successful model for rapidly building governance capacity in resource-constrained settings.

open_problems.problem: Whether the x-risk framing resonates in countries facing more immediate AI-related challenges (labor displacement, surveillance)
open_problems.best_answer: A [2025 arXiv study](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.14040v1) analyzing global media coverage across 12 countries found Global South nations prioritize labor displacement, algorithmic discrimination, and data exploitation over existential risk. [Carnegie](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/04/advancing-a-more-global-agenda-for-trustworthy-artificial-intelligence) argues for a broader framing.
open_problems.source: 'Global Perspectives of AI Risks and Harms,' arXiv, 2025
open_problems.critique: Useful empirical evidence but media coverage is a proxy. No paper convincingly bridges the x-risk and immediate-harms framings. Resources spent on x-risk governance may come at the expense of addressing immediate AI harms.

open_problems.problem: How to design international governance institutions that genuinely share power rather than co-opting Global South participation
open_problems.best_answer: [Brookings](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/ai-in-the-global-south-opportunities-and-challenges-towards-more-inclusive-governance/) proposes moving beyond consultation to genuine co-design with shared steering. [Stanford HAI](https://hai.stanford.edu/policy/moving-beyond-the-term-global-south-in-ai-ethics-and-policy) argues for 'moving beyond the term Global South' entirely. China's WAICO proposal risks becoming a vehicle for Chinese influence rather than genuine power-sharing.
open_problems.source: Brookings, 'AI in the Global South,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Aspirational frameworks without demonstrated designs. AI governance power flows from technical capacity, which remains concentrated. Genuinely power-sharing institutions in technology governance have no historical precedent.

open_problems.problem: How to address the tension between universal governance standards and diverse national contexts
open_problems.best_answer: The [OECD AI Principles](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-artificial-intelligence-papers_dee339a8-en.html) (updated 2024) balance universality with flexibility via non-binding guidelines. The [Tony Blair Institute](https://institute.global/insights/tech-and-digitalisation/how-leaders-in-the-global-south-can-devise-ai-regulation-that-enables-innovation) (2025) advises adapting rather than importing Global North frameworks.
open_problems.source: OECD AI Principles, 2024; TBI, 2025
open_problems.critique: Non-binding preserves flexibility but sacrifices enforceability. The fundamental dilemma remains: universal standards risk cultural imperialism, diverse standards risk regulatory arbitrage.

categories.name: Field Building
categories.description: Growing the AI governance research community — fellowships, training programs, research infrastructure, and talent pipelines.

agendas.name: Landscape Surveys & Research Agendas
agendas.description: Meta-level work mapping the AI governance field, identifying gaps, and setting research priorities.

papers.title: AI Governance: A Research Agenda
papers.description: The most comprehensive attempt to introduce and orient researchers to the space of plausibly important problems in AI governance.

papers.title: AI Model Registries: A Regulatory Review
papers.description: Comprehensive proposal for national AI model registries as a foundational governance tool, developed in collaboration with Gillian Hadfield. Directly influenced US regulatory development.

papers.title: AI governance and strategy: a list of research agendas and work that could be done
papers.description: Catalogs research agendas across 10 governance themes — regulation, compute, corporate, international, misuse, evals, China, infosec, strategy, and post-AGI — with theories of change and specific questions.

papers.title: The longtermist AI governance landscape: a basic overview
papers.description: Maps the longtermist AI governance field into strategy research, tactics research, policy advocacy, and field-building, covering ~15 organizations.

agendas.research_direction: Meta-level work mapping the AI governance field itself — identifying what work exists, where the gaps are, how different efforts relate to each other, and what research priorities should be. This agenda produces the research agendas, landscape overviews, and bibliographic resources that help newcomers navigate the field and help funders allocate resources.
agendas.theory_of_change: A fragmented field with poor legibility wastes effort on duplication and misses important gaps. By making the landscape visible — who is doing what, where the gaps are, what theories of change are being tested — landscape surveys reduce coordination costs and help the community allocate scarce talent and funding more effectively.
agendas.progress: [Dafoe's 2018 research agenda](https://www.governance.ai/research-paper/agenda) remains the foundational document. [Barnard & Robertson (2024)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/WRnT9hGfg3oKfKtXa/ai-governance-and-strategy-a-list-of-research-agendas-and) cataloged research agendas across 10 governance themes. [Clarke (2022)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/ydpo7LcJWhrr2GJrx/the-longtermist-ai-governance-landscape-a-basic-overview) mapped the longtermist governance landscape. [Convergence Analysis](https://www.convergenceanalysis.org)'s AI Model Registries work directly influenced US regulatory development. However, no regularly-updated, comprehensive catalog of AI governance research exists — this project is an attempt to fill that gap.

open_problems.problem: How to maintain a living catalog of a fast-moving field
open_problems.best_answer: The [MIT AI Governance Mapping](https://airisk.mit.edu/ai-governance) project uses LLM-based classification of 1,000+ governance documents from CSET's AGORA archive, with periodic re-snapshots. This automated approach is the most scalable, though it requires ongoing validation.
open_problems.source: MIT AI Risk Initiative, AI Governance Mapping
open_problems.critique: The automated approach scales but LLM classification needs continuous validation (acknowledged by the project). The x-risk governance community has no equivalent — most catalogs are one-off efforts. This project is an attempt to fill that gap.

open_problems.problem: Whether the EA/rationalist community's framing of AI governance is too narrow, missing important work in mainstream political science, international relations, and law
open_problems.best_answer: [Carnegie's 'Advancing a More Global Agenda'](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/04/advancing-a-more-global-agenda-for-trustworthy-artificial-intelligence) (2024) argues that the x-risk framing excludes important governance perspectives from the Global South, labor movements, and democratic theory. The growing body of work in mainstream IR journals and law reviews on AI governance is largely disconnected from the EA/rationalist literature.
open_problems.source: Carnegie, 'Advancing a More Global Agenda,' 2024
open_problems.critique: A valid critique. This catalog itself reflects the bias — most sources are from the EA/rationalist ecosystem, GovAI, and anglophone policy research. Mainstream IR scholars like Kai-Fu Lee, political theorists, and non-English governance researchers are underrepresented.

open_problems.problem: How to evaluate the quality and impact of governance research (not just count it)
open_problems.best_answer: No systematic methodology exists for evaluating AI governance research quality or impact. The closest approximation is citation analysis and policy uptake tracking (e.g., Convergence Analysis's model registries work influencing US policy), but this is done ad hoc rather than systematically.
open_problems.source: No single source
open_problems.critique: A genuine gap. The field counts papers and categorizes them but has no impact evaluation methodology. This matters because governance research that doesn't influence policy is academic exercise.

open_problems.problem: How to make landscape surveys useful to policymakers, not just researchers
open_problems.best_answer: The [CSET AGORA](https://cset.georgetown.edu/) archive and the [OECD AI Policy Observatory](https://oecd.ai/) are the most policymaker-oriented tools, designed for government officials rather than academics. Both use categorization schemes tied to policy domains rather than academic disciplines.
open_problems.source: CSET AGORA; OECD AI Policy Observatory
open_problems.critique: Good tools but aimed at general AI governance, not x-risk specifically. No resource bridges the gap between x-risk governance research and the policy officials who need to act on it.

agendas.name: Open-Source vs. Closed-Source Governance
agendas.description: The debate over whether frontier AI models should be open-weight or closed, and the governance implications of each approach.

papers.title: The Costs and Benefits of Open-Source Generative AI
papers.description: Analysis of the tradeoffs between open and closed AI development, examining proliferation risks against innovation and safety research benefits.

papers.title: Open-Source AI: A Regulatory Review
papers.description: Examines tradeoffs between open-sourcing AI for innovation/transparency versus safety risks of misuse, analyzing policies from US, EU, and China.

papers.title: The Open-Weight Problem
papers.description: Argues open-weight AI models present biosecurity risks due to easily removable safeguards and unmonitorable usage, identifies pretraining interventions as promising.

agendas.research_direction: Navigate the governance implications of open-weight AI models — where model weights are publicly released and anyone can fine-tune, modify, or deploy them without oversight. Research examines the tradeoffs between openness (innovation, transparency, safety research, democratization) and risk (misuse, removal of safeguards, unmonitorable deployment). This was one of the most contested AI governance debates of 2024-2025.
agendas.theory_of_change: Open-weight models fundamentally change the governance landscape. Most governance proposals (RSPs, deployment controls, monitoring) assume a controlled-access model where developers maintain some authority over use. Open weights remove that control. If the benefits of openness (enabling safety research, preventing monopoly, distributing capability) outweigh the risks, governance should protect open development. If the risks are too high, governance should restrict it — at least above certain capability thresholds.
agendas.progress: [RAND](https://www.rand.org) produced a cost-benefit analysis of open-source generative AI. [Convergence Analysis](https://www.convergenceanalysis.org) ([McKernon & Cheng, 2024](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/vCpgHCFzS3xaio2Lm/open-source-ai-a-regulatory-review)) reviewed regulatory approaches across US, EU, and China. The [EU AI Act](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/) includes partial exemptions for open-source models. [Kim (2026)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/y9GkyJ6qfuRqCGTdR/the-open-weight-problem) argued open weights create biosecurity risks. Meta's Llama releases intensified the debate. However, the field lacks empirical evidence on the magnitude of misuse risk from open-weight models — claims on both sides are largely theoretical.

open_problems.problem: At what capability level open-weight release becomes unacceptably risky
open_problems.best_answer: [Casper et al.](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5705186) (2025) documents open-weight models trailing frontier by 6-12 months and approaching critical thresholds. The [AIGL](https://www.aigl.blog/content/files/2025/04/Intolerable-Risk-Threshold-Recommendations-for-Artificial-Intelligence.pdf) attempts to define specific capability levels. But no paper defines a concrete, measurable threshold — the 6-12 month lag is shrinking and capability evaluations are unreliable.
open_problems.source: Casper et al., 'Open Technical Problems,' 2025
open_problems.critique: No concrete measurable threshold has been defined. Any threshold is a moving target. The fundamental problem is unreliable capability evaluations making any threshold-based policy rest on shaky foundations.

open_problems.problem: Whether any governance mechanism can meaningfully control open-weight models once released
open_problems.best_answer: [Casper et al.](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5705186) directly concludes: once weights are released, technical controls are essentially impossible — models can be fine-tuned to remove safeguards, and distribution cannot be recalled. This is a fundamental constraint, not a solvable problem.
open_problems.source: Casper et al., 'Open Technical Problems,' 2025
open_problems.critique: An honest 'no.' Convincing analysis that leaves governance focused on pre-release controls only.

open_problems.problem: How to preserve the benefits of openness (safety research, competition) while managing risks
open_problems.best_answer: [Carnegie](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/07/beyond-open-vs-closed-emerging-consensus-and-key-questions-for-foundation-ai-model-governance) argues for graduated openness with structured access tiers. The [Centre for Future Generations](https://cfg.eu/beyond-the-binary/) extends this to 'Beyond the Binary.'
open_problems.source: Carnegie, 'Beyond Open vs. Closed,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The most promising framing but largely conceptual. Carnegie's 'emerging consensus' may overstate actual agreement. No paper demonstrates how graduated openness works when determined actors circumvent tiers.

open_problems.problem: Whether hardware-level controls (compute governance) can substitute for software-level controls when weights are open
open_problems.best_answer: [Sastry et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.08797) propose hardware-enabled governance but explicitly note these 'could also be applied to unreasonably surveil individuals' and that 'embedding compliance into hardware is not a substitute for strategic enforcement.'
open_problems.source: Sastry et al., 'Computing Power and the Governance of AI,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The most authoritative treatment essentially answers 'no.' Hardware controls complement but don't replace software governance. Declining compute requirements for inference further undermines the approach.

open_problems.problem: How to handle models that are partially open (API access, fine-tuning access, vs. full weight release)
open_problems.best_answer: The [Model Openness Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.13784) (Linux Foundation, 2024) proposes three tiers: Class III (weights), Class II (training code + datasets), Class I (all artifacts). The [OECD](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/ai-openness_02f73362-en.html) (2025) adopts this framework.
open_problems.source: 'Model Openness Framework,' LFAI & Data, 2024
open_problems.critique: Useful taxonomy but a classification system, not a governance framework. It tells you what kind of openness you have but not what obligations attach to each level.

agendas.name: Whistleblower Protections & Lab Transparency
agendas.description: Protecting AI lab employees who raise safety concerns, and mechanisms for transparency about dangerous capabilities.

papers.title: A Right to Warn about Advanced Artificial Intelligence
papers.description: Open letter from current and former AI lab employees calling for protections for workers who raise safety concerns, including the right to voice risk-related concerns to the public.

papers.title: Call on AI Companies: Publish Your Whistleblowing Policies
papers.description: Campaign by 30+ organizations demanding frontier AI companies publicly disclose their whistleblowing policies, finding that 5 of 6 companies don't publish them.

agendas.research_direction: Protect AI lab employees who raise safety concerns from retaliation, and create institutional channels for safety-relevant information to reach regulators and the public. Research covers legal protections, corporate policies, and the broader question of transparency at AI labs.
agendas.theory_of_change: Lab employees are the first to know when safety is being compromised. If they fear retaliation — firing, legal action, NDA enforcement — they stay silent, and dangerous decisions go unchallenged. Whistleblower protections create an information channel from inside labs to external regulators and the public, making it harder for labs to ignore safety concerns behind closed doors.
agendas.progress: The '[Right to Warn](https://rightowarn.ai/)' open letter (2024) by current and former AI lab employees brought the issue to public attention. A campaign by 30+ organizations (2025) found that 5 of 6 major AI companies don't even publish their whistleblowing policies. Some US states and the EU have some protections, but they're untested in the AI context. The departure of key safety researchers from OpenAI in 2024, amid allegations of NDA misuse, was a defining event.

open_problems.problem: How to design protections that work across jurisdictions (AI labs operate globally)
open_problems.best_answer: The [AI Whistleblower Protection Act](https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/1792/text) (US, 2025) and the EU Whistleblowing Directive (with AI Act provisions effective August 2026) are the two most developed frameworks. But no mechanism exists for protecting a whistleblower at a multinational lab who reports to authorities in a different jurisdiction.
open_problems.source: AIWPA, S.1792, 2025; EU AI Act whistleblowing provisions
open_problems.critique: Both address domestic protections but cross-jurisdictional protection for AI whistleblowers has not been seriously addressed. A genuine gap.

open_problems.problem: How to handle legitimate trade secrets vs. safety-relevant information that the public needs to know
open_problems.best_answer: The AIWPA defines protected disclosures as those related to 'AI security vulnerabilities' or 'substantial and specific dangers' to public safety, health, or national security. The [Princeton CITP](https://blog.citp.princeton.edu/2025/06/03/whistleblower-protection-act/) examines the boundary.
open_problems.source: AIWPA; Princeton CITP, 'What the AIWPA Would Mean,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The definitions are broad enough but the trade-secret/safety boundary is inherently blurry in AI (model architecture can be both). No clear rubric for regulators exists.

open_problems.problem: Whether voluntary corporate policies are sufficient or legislation is required
open_problems.best_answer: The '[Right to Warn](https://righttowarn.ai/)' letter (2024) by 13 current/former lab employees, endorsed by Hinton, Bengio, and Russell, directly argued voluntary policies are insufficient, pointing to broad NDAs and lack of anonymous channels. Subsequent legislative action (AIWPA) reflects consensus that legislation is needed.
open_problems.source: 'A Right to Warn,' 2024
open_problems.critique: A powerful firsthand account, but advocacy rather than analysis. The empirical question — whether corporate policies demonstrably fail — hasn't been rigorously studied because relevant cases are recent and confidential.

open_problems.problem: How to protect whistleblowers from informal retaliation (reputation damage, career consequences) in addition to formal retaliation
open_problems.best_answer: The AIWPA prohibits 'directly or indirectly' discharging, demoting, threatening, blacklisting, harassing, or 'in any other manner discriminating.' But informal retaliation (exclusion from projects, diminished references, industry blacklisting) is extremely difficult to prove.
