open_problems.critique: Broad language is necessary but the enforcement challenge is real. No paper proposes effective mechanisms for detecting and remedying informal retaliation in AI's tight-knit labor market.

open_problems.problem: How to create regulatory channels that can receive and act on safety disclosures
open_problems.best_answer: The AIWPA provides for disclosures to Congress, federal inspectors general, and regulatory agencies. But [The Future Society](https://thefuturesociety.org/ai-whistleblowers/) argues effective channels require technical expertise that current agencies largely lack.
open_problems.source: Future Society, 'Why Whistleblowers Are Critical,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The AIWPA creates channels but doesn't ensure they have technical capacity to evaluate disclosures. An alignment failure report requires deep expertise most agencies don't possess.

agendas.name: Autonomous Weapons (LAWS)
agendas.description: Governance of lethal autonomous weapons systems — UN CCW negotiations, military AI ethics, and preventive regulation.

papers.title: Autonomous Weapons Systems: A Roadmap for Continued Progress
papers.description: Roadmap for governing autonomous weapons with concrete policy recommendations, from one of the leading experts on military AI governance.

papers.title: Military Artificial Intelligence as Contributor to Global Catastrophic Risk
papers.description: Examines whether military AI applications constitute global catastrophic risks, focusing on lethal autonomous weapons and AI-nuclear command intersections.

papers.title: Militaries Are Going Autonomous. But Will AI Lead to New Wars?
papers.description: Literature review examining whether lethal autonomous weapons will alter geopolitical decision-making and international stability.

papers.title: Beyond a Human 'In the Loop': Strategic Stability and AI
papers.description: Finds AI destabilizes nuclear deterrence by compressing escalation timelines and enhancing ISR, but also enhances early warning — a stability-instability paradox.

agendas.research_direction: Govern lethal autonomous weapons systems (LAWS) — weapons that can select and engage targets without meaningful human control. Research covers the [UN Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons](https://www.un.org/disarmament/the-convention-on-certain-conventional-weapons/) (CCW) negotiations, definitions of autonomy and human control, verification of autonomous weapons limits, and the intersection of military AI with broader AI governance.
agendas.theory_of_change: Autonomous weapons lower the cost and political barrier to using lethal force, potentially destabilizing international security. If weapons can kill without human decision-making, the speed of conflict escalation increases and accountability decreases. International regulation — whether a ban, a requirement for meaningful human control, or transparency measures — can reduce these risks. Military AI governance also intersects with broader AI governance because military R&D drives capability development.
agendas.progress: UN CCW discussions on LAWS have been ongoing since 2014 but have produced no binding agreement. [FLI](https://futureoflife.org) has campaigned for a ban on autonomous weapons. [Scharre's work](https://www.cnas.org/publications/reports/autonomous-weapons-systems-a-roadmap-for-continued-progress) at [CNAS](https://www.cnas.org) provides detailed policy roadmaps. [Maas et al. (2022)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/dCb8tWsAmbYPSiqYT/military-artificial-intelligence-as-contributor-to-global) framed military AI as a global catastrophic risk. However, major military powers (US, China, Russia) have resisted binding restrictions, and autonomous military systems are being developed and deployed without international regulation. The 2026 literature review (Rorvig) finds no consensus on whether LAWS increase or decrease conflict risk.

open_problems.problem: Whether a ban on autonomous weapons is politically feasible given major power resistance
open_problems.best_answer: The December 2024 UN General Assembly resolution on LAWS passed 166-3-15, demonstrating overwhelming support. But the [Arms Control Association](https://www.armscontrol.org/act/2025-01/features/geopolitics-and-regulation-autonomous-weapons-systems) identifies three blocs, and the three most important military AI developers (US, Russia, China) resist binding prohibitions.
open_problems.source: Arms Control Association, 'Geopolitics and Regulation of Autonomous Weapons,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The 166-3 vote overstates consensus because it's non-binding and the three most important states opposed/abstained. A ban is infeasible while major powers see strategic value. Clear-eyed analysis.

open_problems.problem: How to define 'meaningful human control' in a way that is both operationally useful and verifiable
open_problems.best_answer: A [2025 Ethics & IT paper](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10676-025-09874-x) examines MHC through collective moral responsibility and institutional design. The CCW rolling text (Nov 2024) converges on 'predictable, reliable, traceable, and explainable.' But the US and Russia reject 'meaningful human control' entirely.
open_problems.source: 'LAWS: Meaningful Human Control,' Ethics & IT, 2025; CCW Rolling Text, 2024
open_problems.critique: Sophisticated philosophical analysis but the states that matter most reject the concept entirely. Alternative formulations ('predictable, reliable, traceable') may not capture what MHC advocates want.

open_problems.problem: Whether autonomous weapons governance should be separate from or integrated with broader AI governance
open_problems.best_answer: [SIPRI](https://www.sipri.org/publications/2025/other-publications/autonomous-weapon-systems-and-ai-enabled-decision-support-systems-military-targeting-comparison-and) (2025) recommends separate multilateral processes for AI decision-support systems while building on AWS governance lessons. The dual-track approach (REAIM summits + UN) is the emerging practice.
open_problems.source: SIPRI, 'AWS and AI-DSS in Military Targeting,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Most analytically rigorous but separate processes risk fragmentation. The dual-use nature makes clean separation increasingly unrealistic.

open_problems.problem: How to handle the dual-use problem — many military AI capabilities come from civilian AI research
open_problems.best_answer: [Carnegie](https://carnegieendowment.org/research/2024/08/understanding-the-global-debate-on-lethal-autonomous-weapons-systems-an-indian-perspective) examines how developing countries navigate dual-use tensions. The emerging consensus is a two-tiered approach: prohibitions on some autonomous weapons, regulations on others.
open_problems.source: Carnegie, 'Understanding the Global Debate on LAWS: An Indian Perspective,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The two-tiered approach is pragmatic but requires drawing lines when the underlying technology is identical. The Indian perspective valuably highlights how non-aligned nations see the problem differently.

open_problems.problem: Whether autonomous weapons make nuclear deterrence more or less stable
open_problems.best_answer: The [Arms Control Association](https://www.armscontrol.org/issue-briefs/2024-011/beyond-the-loop) (2024) finds AI destabilizes deterrence by compressing escalation timelines, enhancing ISR that threatens second-strike forces, and creating autonomous escalation pathways. But AI also enhances early warning — a stability-instability paradox.
open_problems.source: 'Beyond a Human In the Loop,' Arms Control Association, 2024
open_problems.critique: Reaches the nuanced conclusion that the net effect is ambiguous — AI simultaneously stabilizes and destabilizes depending on application. Analytically honest but policy-unhelpful. Empirical testing is obviously impossible.

agendas.name: AI Safety Institutes
agendas.description: Analysis of government AI Safety Institutes (UK AISI, US AISI) — their mandates, capabilities, and effectiveness.

papers.title: UK AI Safety Institute Approach to Evaluations
papers.description: UK AISI's framework for evaluating frontier AI models before and after deployment, including methodology for dangerous capability assessments.

papers.title: AI Safety Evaluations: A Regulatory Review
papers.description: Examines AI safety evaluation techniques and regulatory approaches across US, EU, China, and UK, concluding that independent third-party assessment must be rapidly developed.

agendas.research_direction: Analyze government AI Safety Institutes — their mandates, capabilities, effectiveness, and institutional design. The [UK AISI](https://www.aisi.gov.uk) (established 2023) and US AISI (established 2024, at NIST) are the primary subjects, along with emerging AISIs in other countries. Research asks whether government evaluation capacity can keep pace with private-sector AI development and what institutional features make AISIs effective.
agendas.theory_of_change: Government evaluation capacity is necessary for credible AI governance. Without it, regulators must rely on labs' self-assessments, which are compromised by conflicts of interest. AISIs provide independent technical capacity to evaluate frontier models, set standards, and advise policymakers. If AISIs can maintain technical talent and access to frontier models, they become the institutional backbone of AI safety regulation.
agendas.progress: [UK AISI](https://www.aisi.gov.uk) has conducted evaluations of several frontier models and published methodology reports. US AISI at NIST is developing the [AI Risk Management Framework](https://www.nist.gov/artificial-intelligence/executive-order-safe-secure-and-trustworthy-artificial-intelligence). However, AISIs face severe challenges: limited pre-deployment access to models (labs often provide only limited access), difficulty competing with private-sector salaries for technical talent, and political vulnerability (the US AISI's future is uncertain under different administrations). [Convergence Analysis](https://www.convergenceanalysis.org) (2024) found existing evaluation techniques insufficient across all jurisdictions.

open_problems.problem: How to attract and retain technical talent when private-sector salaries are 3-5x higher
open_problems.best_answer: [All Tech Is Human](https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/the-global-landscape-of-ai-safety-institutes) (2025) documents that AISIs have explored joint university appointments, research sabbaticals, and rotating fellowships. The appeal to public service mission and unique access to frontier models are key non-monetary draws.
open_problems.source: All Tech Is Human, 'Global Landscape of AI Safety Institutes,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Reasonable strategies but limited in scale. No AISI has demonstrated ability to attract and retain top-tier researchers at scale. Rotating fellowships mean institutional knowledge is continually lost.

open_problems.problem: Whether AISIs should have legal authority to mandate pre-deployment evaluations (currently access is voluntary)
open_problems.best_answer: The UK Labour government's AI Opportunities Action Plan (January 2025) includes proposals for statutory authority. The [Stanford AI Index](https://hai.stanford.edu/assets/files/hai_ai-index-report-2025_chapter6_final.pdf) (2025) surveys the spectrum from purely voluntary (US) to quasi-mandatory (UK). No country has yet granted mandatory evaluation authority.
open_problems.source: Stanford HAI, 'AI Index Report Ch. 6,' 2025
open_problems.critique: The UK statutory proposal is most advanced but not yet implemented. No paper analyzes whether mandatory authority would enhance or undermine AISI effectiveness through loss of industry cooperation.

open_problems.problem: How to make AISIs politically durable across changes in government
open_problems.best_answer: [CSIS](https://www.csis.org/analysis/ai-safety-institute-international-network-next-steps-and-recommendations) (2024) recommends diversified funding, statutory enshrinement, and international embedding. The UK's move toward statutory status is the most concrete attempt. But the US AISI's status shifted dramatically with the 2025 administration change despite bipartisan origins.
open_problems.source: CSIS, 'AISI Network Next Steps,' 2024
open_problems.critique: The US experience demonstrates the vulnerability: statutory status provides legal but not political durability. No paper addresses the deeper political economy problem.

open_problems.problem: Whether a network of national AISIs can coordinate effectively, or whether international evaluation capacity is needed
open_problems.best_answer: The [AISI Network's first multilateral exercise](https://thefuturesociety.org/aisi-report/) (November 2024) and $11M in joint funding for synthetic content research provide early evidence. The [OECD](https://oecd.ai/en/wonk/ai-safety-institute-networks-role-global-ai-governance) examines integration into the broader ecosystem.
open_problems.source: Future Society, 'Weaving a Safety Net,' 2024; OECD analysis, 2025
open_problems.critique: $11M is trivial compared to frontier lab R&D. Coordination on synthetic content is low-hanging fruit — coordinating on dangerous capability responses is untested. China's absence fundamentally limits coverage.

open_problems.problem: How to evaluate models for risks that are not yet well-understood
open_problems.best_answer: [UK AISI](https://www.aisi.gov.uk/blog/advanced-ai-evaluations-may-update) (2025) demonstrates the most advanced approach: periodic evaluation for dangerous capabilities with adaptive methods. The [International AI Safety Report](https://internationalaisafetyreport.org/publication/international-ai-safety-report-2025) acknowledges 'safety evaluation is dynamic.'
open_problems.source: UK AISI, 'Advanced AI Evaluations,' 2025
open_problems.critique: State of the art but candidly limited: you cannot design evaluations for capabilities you haven't imagined. The 'evaluation gap' between tests and real-world behavior means unknown risks remain fundamentally unaddressable.

agendas.name: Insurance & Liability Frameworks
agendas.description: Using tort law, strict liability, and mandatory insurance to incentivize AI safety investments and compensate for AI-caused harms.

papers.title: Tort Law Can Play an Important Role in Mitigating AI Risk
papers.description: Argues that strict liability and punitive damages could incentivize AI developers to prioritize safety by forcing companies to internalize the risks.

papers.title: AI-Relevant Regulation: Insurance in Safety-Critical Industries
papers.description: Examines insurance requirements in nuclear, aviation, space, and technology sectors to explore how similar mechanisms might govern AI development risks.

papers.title: Liability and Insurance for Catastrophic Losses: The Nuclear Power Precedent and Lessons for AI
papers.description: Proposes mandatory insurance for Critical AI Occurrences drawing on the Price-Anderson Act model. Argues the nuclear precedent is the best available framework.

agendas.research_direction: Use economic mechanisms — tort liability, strict liability, mandatory insurance, punitive damages — to create financial incentives for AI safety. Research draws on how these mechanisms work in other safety-critical industries (nuclear, pharmaceutical, aviation) and asks how they should be adapted for AI.
agendas.theory_of_change: If AI developers face financial consequences for harm — through lawsuits, insurance premiums, or regulatory fines — they internalize the costs of safety failures. This creates market-based incentives for safety investment without requiring regulators to understand the technical details of AI systems. Insurance companies, in particular, have expertise in risk assessment and strong incentives to accurately price risk.
agendas.progress: [Weil (2024)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/epKBmiyLpZWWFEYDb/tort-law-can-play-an-important-role-in-mitigating-ai-risk) made the case for tort law as an AI governance mechanism. Research on insurance in safety-critical industries (2023) examined analogies from nuclear, aviation, and space sectors. Investigation into AI insurance feasibility (2024) found frontier labs are poor candidates (risks too novel and catastrophic to price) but downstream users are viable. No country has yet implemented mandatory AI insurance or a comprehensive AI liability framework, though the [EU AI Act](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/) includes some liability provisions.

open_problems.problem: How to assign liability when AI systems are built on other AI systems (foundation model providers vs. fine-tuners vs. deployers)
open_problems.best_answer: Singapore's IMDA proposed a 'shared responsibility' approach (May 2024) allocating liability across developers, deployers, and cloud providers. [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA3084-1.html) (2025) examines how US tort liability chains apply to layered systems.
open_problems.source: RAND, 'US Tort Liability for Large-Scale AI Damages,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Shared responsibility is sensible but operationally complex — determining which layer caused harm may be technically impossible. No framework has been tested in actual multi-layer AI litigation.

open_problems.problem: Whether catastrophic AI risks can be meaningfully insured (insurers may refuse to cover existential risks)
open_problems.best_answer: [Heim et al.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.06673) (2024) propose mandatory insurance for Critical AI Occurrences, drawing on the Price-Anderson Act (nuclear). Weil argues truly catastrophic scenarios are uninsurable, requiring punitive damages in 'near-miss' cases as a substitute.
open_problems.source: Heim et al., 'Liability and Insurance for Catastrophic Losses,' arXiv, 2024
open_problems.critique: The nuclear analogy has limits: nuclear accidents have physical containment while AI catastrophes could be non-localizable. The near-miss liability proposal is creative but untested. Best available framework.

open_problems.problem: How to handle liability for open-weight models where the original developer has no control over downstream use
open_problems.best_answer: [Casper et al.](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5705186) documents irreversible release making liability impossible. [Lawfare](https://www.lawfaremedia.org/article/why-liability-and-insurance-won-t-save-ai--lessons-from-cyber-insurance) argues the problem is analogous to cyber insurance failures.
open_problems.source: Casper et al., 'Open Technical Problems,' 2025; Lawfare, 2025
open_problems.critique: Convincing that post-release liability is nearly impossible. But no workable alternative beyond pre-release liability, which chills open-source development. Fundamentally unsolved.

open_problems.problem: Whether strict liability or negligence standards are more appropriate for AI
open_problems.best_answer: [Weil](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4694006) (2024) argues for strict liability + mandatory insurance. [Lawfare](https://www.lawfaremedia.org/article/negligence-liability-for-ai-developers) counters that if developers can't avoid harms cost-efficiently, strict liability is just a deterrent penalty. The bipartisan AI LEAD Act (S. 2937, 2025) would create federal strict liability.
open_problems.source: Weil, 'Tort Law as a Tool,' 2024; AI LEAD Act, 2025
open_problems.critique: Well-joined debate with strong arguments on both sides. The AI LEAD Act may provide a real-world test.

open_problems.problem: How to prevent liability regimes from favoring large companies that can afford legal departments and insurance
open_problems.best_answer: The [Oxford/Institute for Law & AI](https://law-ai.org/insuring-emerging-risks-from-ai/) (2024) proposes tiered liability requirements calibrated to model capability and deployment scale.
open_problems.source: 'Insuring Emerging Risks from AI,' Oxford, 2024
open_problems.critique: Sensible but operationalizing tiers is difficult. Small developers building on large models face an attribution problem. Any regime rigorous enough to address real risks will create barriers favoring incumbents.

agendas.name: Democratic Governance of AI
agendas.description: Incorporating public input into AI governance decisions through citizen assemblies, deliberation, and democratic safeguards.

papers.title: Why Most Efforts Towards 'Democratic AI' Fall Short
papers.description: Critiques current 'democratic AI' initiatives as providing only symbolic participation while preserving corporate authority, calling for genuinely enforceable democratic institutions.

agendas.research_direction: Incorporate public input into AI governance decisions that affect society — through citizen assemblies, public deliberation, participatory processes, and democratic oversight of AI development. Research asks whether existing 'democratic AI' initiatives (like OpenAI's Democratic Inputs program) are meaningful or merely performative, and what genuinely democratic AI governance would look like.
agendas.theory_of_change: AI governance decisions — who gets to develop powerful AI, what risks are acceptable, how benefits are distributed — are fundamentally political questions that affect everyone. If these decisions are made by a small group of technologists and investors, the outcomes may not reflect broader social values. Democratic participation creates legitimacy, surfaces diverse perspectives on risk, and reduces the chance that AI governance serves narrow interests.
agendas.progress: [Haimes (2025)](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/AjW6dwWMueHB8Q2s4/why-most-efforts-towards-democratic-ai-fall-short) provided the most pointed critique of current 'democratic AI' initiatives, arguing they provide symbolic participation while preserving corporate authority. Some governments have conducted citizen assemblies on AI (e.g., UK's public attitudes work). However, genuinely democratic AI governance remains largely aspirational. The fundamental challenge is that meaningful participation requires technical understanding that most citizens lack, and that the speed of AI development outpaces deliberative processes.

open_problems.problem: How to make public deliberation on AI governance meaningful rather than performative
open_problems.best_answer: '[Democratizing AI Governance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.08651)' (2025) uses France's Citizens' Convention and Brazil's AI strategy as case studies. [Carnegie](https://carnegieendowment.org/posts/2025/07/how-ai-can-unlock-public-wisdom-and-revitalize-democratic-governance) proposes using AI itself to facilitate structured deliberation.
open_problems.source: 'Democratizing AI Governance,' arXiv, 2025
open_problems.critique: France and Brazil provide useful evidence but both have strong participatory traditions that may not generalize. Using AI to facilitate AI deliberation raises obvious circularity concerns. No paper shows public deliberation has meaningfully altered AI governance outcomes at scale.

open_problems.problem: How to handle the technical complexity barrier — citizens can't easily evaluate AI safety claims
open_problems.best_answer: The same paper proposes intermediary institutions, citizen juries with expert briefings, and structured deliberation. The '[Last Vote](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.13432)' framework allocates decision domains to actors with appropriate expertise.
open_problems.source: 'Democratizing AI Governance,' 2025; 'The Last Vote,' 2025
open_problems.critique: Citizen juries are well-established but untested on decisions as complex as AI safety. The risk is that expert presentations predetermine outcomes, reproducing rather than resolving the complexity barrier.

open_problems.problem: Whether the pace of AI development is compatible with deliberative democratic processes
open_problems.best_answer: '[AI: Challenges for Democracy](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19452829.2025.2518307)' (2025) identifies the temporal mismatch and argues for standing institutions with pre-authorized decision frameworks rather than case-by-case deliberation.
open_problems.source: 'AI: Challenges for Democracy,' J. Human Development, 2025
open_problems.critique: The standing-institution proposal shifts from 'can democracy keep up?' to 'can democratic legitimacy be pre-delegated?' Essentially an argument for technocratic governance with democratic authorization — which many democratic theorists would challenge.

open_problems.problem: How to design democratic institutions for AI governance that are resistant to capture by industry
open_problems.best_answer: '[The Question of AI and Democracy](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13347-025-00904-6)' (Philosophy & Technology, 2025) provides a typology of four governance approaches and their capture vulnerability. Honestly acknowledges information asymmetry makes some industry influence inevitable.
open_problems.source: 'The Question of AI and Democracy,' Philosophy & Technology, 2025
open_problems.critique: Useful typology but none of the four categories offers a convincing capture solution. Information asymmetry makes some degree of influence structural. No known institutional solution.

open_problems.problem: Whether democratic input should influence technical safety decisions or only distributional/political ones
open_problems.best_answer: '[Democratizing AI Governance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.08651)' argues the technical/political boundary is itself politically contested — decisions framed as purely technical embed value choices requiring democratic accountability. [Nay](https://law.stanford.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Aligning-AI-Agents-with-Humans-through-Law-as-Information.pdf) proposes democratically produced law as the alignment target.
open_problems.source: 'Democratizing AI Governance,' 2025; Nay, 'Law as Information,' Stanford, 2025
open_problems.critique: Well-established STS argument but doesn't provide operational guidance on which specific decisions should involve public input. Law evolves slowly relative to AI, and many safety decisions are too granular for legislation.
